perDomain.title: App shell, navigation & auth entry
perDomain.summary:
The macOS and iOS WaiComputer app shells share a solid auth foundation (session restore, Keychain/file token storage, login, register, error display, Sentry init, network monitor) but diverge significantly in three clusters.

AUTH ENTRY GAPS (all PORTABLE, all in AuthView.swift + WaiComputerApp.swift): iOS is missing magic-link request + confirmation state, forgot-password flow, the third 'Magic Link' tab in the auth picker, locale-aware Terms/Privacy URLs (/ru variants), and the deep-link handler for waicomputer://auth/verify tokens. Password validation also shows a minimum-length discrepancy (iOS enforces 6 chars; backend requires 8; macOS enforces 8 with inline hints).

ONBOARDING GAPS: macOS has a two-phase onboarding (pre-auth: permissions/language/hotkey; post-auth: voice setup) while iOS has a single-phase flow with no language-picker slide and no page-progress persistence across restarts. The accessibility, system-audio, hotkey, and dictation-sandbox slides are correctly absent on iOS (PLATFORM_LIMITED). The language/locale picker slide is a meaningful PORTABLE gap.

NAVIGATION / SHELL GAPS: iOS is missing the Trash section (soft-delete + restore), the in-app microphone-permission banner (non-blocking toast overlay), billing-region sync post-login, and the appearance-mode and accent-color preferences wired to the app scene. Dictation History and Dictionary sections are correctly absent (PLATFORM_LIMITED). The menu bar extra, Dock management, Sparkle auto-update, TCC migration, and NSMenu commands are all NOT_APPLICABLE on iOS.

Highest-priority portable gaps: magic link auth (S), deep-link URL handler (M), forgot password (S), billing region sync (M), microphone permission banner (M), language onboarding slide (M), Trash section (M).

gaps.name: Auth flow: Magic link (passwordless) request
gaps.risk: Low — small UI addition; backend already wired

verdict.capabilityName: Auth flow: Magic link (passwordless) request
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. macOS AuthView (MacContentView.swift:1485-1757) implements a three-tab AuthMode enum (.login, .register, .magicLink), renders the "Magic Link" tab via WaiTabBar, calls appState.requestMagicLink(email:acceptedLegalTerms:) (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:926), and shows magicLinkSentView on success. iOS AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift:222-485) has no magicLinkSent property and no requestMagicLink() method. iOS AuthView (AuthView.swift:1-134) has only a two-segment Picker (Login/Register) with no magic link path whatsoever. No onOpenURL deep-link handler for waicomputer://magic?token= exists anywhere in the iOS source tree. The shared kit (APIClient.swift:551-579) already exposes both requestMagicLink() and verifyMagicLink(), making the network layer fully available. The sole platform dependency in the macOS implementation that does not exist on iOS is .toggleStyle(.checkbox) at MacContentView.swift:1664, used for the legal consent Toggle in the magic link tab; iOS requires .toggleStyle(.switch) instead. The Image(nsImage: NSApp.applicationIconImage) at line 1508 is in the decorative header, not the magic link logic. WaiTabBar (DesignSystem.swift:374) is pure SwiftUI with no AppKit dependency. The iOS DesignSystem.swift is a separate file so Palette/Typography/Spacing tokens must be checked for completeness, but the auth logic itself is straightforwardly portable: add magicLinkSent + requestMagicLink to iOS AppState, add a third tab to AuthView, and substitute .toggleStyle(.switch) for .checkbox.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSApp.applicationIconImage (decorative header only, not blocking — replace with Image(systemName:) or asset on iOS)
.toggleStyle(.checkbox) (macOS-only; legal consent Toggle must use .toggleStyle(.switch) on iOS)

gaps.name: Auth flow: Magic link sent confirmation state ('Check your email')
gaps.risk: Low — piggybacks on magic link gap

verdict.capabilityName: Auth flow: Magic link sent confirmation state ('Check your email')
verdict.reason: Gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. macOS MacAppState (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:592) has `@Published var magicLinkSent = false`; MacContentView.swift:1685-1703 renders a pure-SwiftUI VStack (envelope icon, "Check your email" text, email address, "Send again" button) conditioned on `appState.magicLinkSent`. iOS AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift:224-485) has no `magicLinkSent` property and no magic link auth mode at all — AuthView only offers login and register with email+password. A grep of shared/WaiComputerKit returns zero hits for magicLinkSent, magicLink, or sendMagicLink. MacContentView.swift imports only `SwiftUI` and `WaiComputerKit` (line 1-2); the magicLinkSentView uses only SwiftUI primitives (Image(systemName:), Text, Button, VStack, WaiGhostButtonStyle) with no NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, or AX symbols. The feature can be ported to iOS by: (1) adding `@Published var magicLinkSent = false` to iOS AppState, (2) adding a magic link tab to iOS AuthView, (3) wiring the send-magic-link API call, and (4) copying the confirmation VStack — all using standard SwiftUI and the existing shared APIClient.

gaps.name: Auth flow: Forgot password / password reset request
gaps.risk: Low — isolated to AuthView + AppState extension

verdict.capabilityName: Auth flow: Forgot password / password reset request
verdict.reason: Gap is real and confirmed by direct code inspection. macOS MacAppState (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:593) has @Published var passwordResetSent = false and func requestPasswordReset(email:locale:) async (lines 952-966) which calls the shared APIClient. MacContentView.swift:1629-1641 renders the "Forgot password?" ghost button in login mode, and lines 1536-1546 show the confirmation text gated on passwordResetSent. iOS AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift:224-485) has neither the passwordResetSent property nor the requestPasswordReset method. iOS AuthView.swift (all 134 lines) has no forgot-password button and no passwordResetSent handling. The shared kit already provides the full network layer: APIClient.requestPasswordReset(email:locale:) at shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:572-575 posts to /api/auth/forgot-password with no platform-specific imports. The entire implementation chain — state property, async method, SwiftUI button, confirmation text — uses only standard Swift/SwiftUI/Foundation APIs with zero macOS-only symbols. Porting requires: (1) add @Published var passwordResetSent = false to iOS AppState, (2) add func requestPasswordReset(email:locale:) async to iOS AppState mirroring the macOS version, (3) add the "Forgot password?" Button and passwordResetSent confirmation Text to iOS AuthView in login mode.

gaps.name: Auth form: password validation hint (min length, mismatch text)
gaps.risk: Low — purely UI; aligning min-length may be a security hygiene fix

verdict.capabilityName: Auth form: password validation hint (min length, mismatch text)
verdict.reason: Gap confirmed. macOS MacContentView.swift:1471 defines `minPasswordLength = 8`; lines 1609–1624 render an `authFieldHint` showing "At least 8 characters" (conditionally isError) and "Passwords don't match" (always isError) using only Text + .font(Typography.caption) + .foregroundStyle(Palette.recording / Palette.textSecondary) + .frame — pure SwiftUI. iOS AuthView.swift:107 silently enforces `password.count >= 6` (wrong threshold, not 8) and shows no hint text at any line. No other iOS file provides these hints, and the shared kit has no password validation UI. Palette.recording and Palette.textSecondary are both present in ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift (lines 51, 58), so every token the hint uses is already available on iOS. The only macOS-exclusive pattern in the nearby auth form is .toggleStyle(.checkbox) at line 1664 (inside legalConsentRow, not in the hint path), which iOS AuthView already replaces with .toggleStyle(.switch). No blocking platform dependency exists in the hint implementation itself.

gaps.name: Auth form: locale-aware Terms/Privacy links (/ru variants)
gaps.risk: Low — one conditional string substitution

verdict.capabilityName: Auth form: locale-aware Terms/Privacy links (/ru variants)
verdict.reason: Gap is real and the fix is fully portable. iOS AuthView.swift:60-62 hardcodes https://wai.computer/terms and https://wai.computer/privacy with no locale switch. macOS MacAuthView (MacContentView.swift:1474-1482) computes termsOfServiceURL and privacyPolicyURL by checking authLocale (line 1737), which reads LanguageManager.preferredLocale.language.languageCode — LanguageManager lives in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift and is already imported by the iOS target (import WaiComputerKit is present in AuthView.swift:2). The entire locale-switch block (authLocale computed property, two URL computed properties, SwiftUI Link usage in legalConsentRow) uses only SwiftUI and LanguageManager — no AppKit, NSApp, NSWindow, NSPanel, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Sparkle, or any other iOS-unavailable symbol. The one macOS-only symbol in the surrounding struct — NSApp.applicationIconImage at line 1508 — is in the app-icon header of MacAuthView and is entirely unrelated to the legal link logic; it would not be ported. The fix for iOS is to inject LanguageManager (already available) and replace the two hardcoded URL strings with the same ru/en conditional already used on macOS.

gaps.name: Deep-link handling: waicomputer://auth/verify magic-link token
gaps.risk: Medium — requires Info.plist URL scheme entry; test that universal-link conflicts do not arise

verdict.capabilityName: Deep-link handling: waicomputer://auth/verify magic-link token
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed at three layers. First, the iOS Info.plist (/Users/mikwiseman/Documents/Code/wai-computer/ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Info.plist) has no CFBundleURLTypes entry; the waicomputer:// scheme is not registered. The macOS Info.plist at /Users/mikwiseman/Documents/Code/wai-computer/macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Info.plist:27-34 registers the scheme. Second, WaiComputerApp.swift (iOS, lines 208-220) has no .onOpenURL modifier on the WindowGroup; the macOS equivalent lives at WaiComputerMacApp.swift:120-128. Third, iOS AppState (lines 224-485) has no requestMagicLink, no handleIncomingURL, no verifyMagicLink call, and AuthView.swift offers only email/password login and register with no magic-link path. The macOS handleIncomingURL body (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:968-995) uses only URLComponents, apiClient.verifyMagicLink (shared kit, APIClient.swift:577-579), and session persistence via KeychainHelper (also shared kit) — zero macOS-only symbols inside the handler itself. The macOS-only part is the delivery path: NSApplicationDelegate.application(_:open:) at line 515 forwards via NotificationCenter because .onOpenURL is unreliable for already-running macOS apps; on iOS .onOpenURL on WindowGroup is the standard and reliable mechanism. The fix requires: (1) add CFBundleURLTypes/waicomputer to iOS Info.plist, (2) add .onOpenURL { url in Task { await appState.handleIncomingURL(url) } } to WaiComputerApp.body, (3) add handleIncomingURL and requestMagicLink to iOS AppState using KeychainHelper (already used there) rather than SessionStore, and (4) expose a magic-link request button in AuthView. All building blocks (verifyMagicLink, requestMagicLink) exist in the shared APIClient. No platform-limiting symbols are present in the handler logic.

gaps.name: Post-login: billing region sync to server
gaps.risk: Medium — incorrect region stamp leads to wrong billing/currency at checkout

verdict.capabilityName: Post-login: billing region sync to server
verdict.reason: Gap confirmed at two levels. First, iOS WaiComputerApp.swift:452-464 loadCurrentUser() has no call to any region-sync function — the call to syncDownloadRegionToServerIfNeeded() present at macOS WaiComputerMacApp.swift:1161 is entirely absent. Second, the iOS Info.plist (/ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Info.plist) does not contain the WAIDownloadRegion key at all, whereas the macOS Info.plist sets it via the build variable $(WAI_DOWNLOAD_REGION) (line 49). No equivalent in the shared kit or elsewhere on iOS was found. The macOS implementation uses only Bundle.main.object(forInfoDictionaryKey:), BillingDisplayRegion (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Billing/BillingModels.swift:8), APIClient.getSettings() and APIClient.updateSettings() (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:616) — all of these are already in the shared kit and fully available to iOS. No macOS-only symbols are present in the sync path. To close the gap: add WAIDownloadRegion to the iOS Info.plist (keyed to a build variable), and add a call to the same sync logic inside WaiComputerApp.swift:loadCurrentUser() after the successful getCurrentUser() call. The entire supporting type surface (BillingDisplayRegion, UpdateSettingsRequest.region, APIClient) is portable with no hidden platform dependency.

gaps.name: Two-phase onboarding: pre-auth (permission/language/hotkey) + post-auth (voice setup)
gaps.risk: Medium — state-machine change to AppState; must not regress existing onboarding for users who already completed it

verdict.capabilityName: Two-phase onboarding: pre-auth (permission/language/hotkey) + post-auth (voice setup)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed as described. macOS MacContentView.swift:13-21 has a genuine four-state gate gated on hasCompletedPreAuthOnboarding (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:597) and hasCompletedPostAuthOnboarding (line 598). iOS ContentView.swift:12-14 uses a single hasCompletedOnboarding flag (WaiComputerApp.swift:230) with no pre/post-auth split. The macOS OnboardingPage.swift:39-45 maps preAuth to [welcome, valueProps, permission, languages, hotkey] and postAuth to [sandbox, voiceSetup]. iOS OnboardingPage.swift has only [welcome, record, transcribe, permission, voiceSetup] — no hotkey page, no languages page, no dictation sandbox, and all five run before auth with voiceSetup in the wrong phase. The structural split (two boolean flags + two-phase gate) is SwiftUI-portable in isolation, but the content of the pre-auth flow is not: the hotkey picker (OnboardingHotkeyPickerSlide) depends on DictationManager.updateHotkey which is a macOS-global-hotkey concept with no iOS equivalent; the dictation sandbox (OnboardingDictationSandboxSlide) exercises text-insertion via CGEvent.post, which is impossible on iOS. The OnboardingView itself imports Carbon (line 3), uses MacInputPermission.Status (lines 13, 320, 364, 591-592), MacPrivacySettings (lines 100, 245, 455, 479, 551), and NSColor (lines 56, 907). Porting the postAuth voiceSetup deferral to iOS is feasible in pure SwiftUI, but porting the full pre-auth content (hotkey + languages + dictation sandbox) is blocked by platform-unavailable APIs.
hiddenPlatformDeps: DictationHotkey / global hotkey registration (OnboardingHotkeyPickerSlide — no iOS equivalent)
DictationManager.updateHotkey (macOS global hotkey management, no iOS equivalent)

gaps.name: Onboarding: language/locale picker slide
gaps.risk: Medium — touches onboarding state machine and needs LanguageManager wired into iOS environment objects

verdict.capabilityName: Onboarding: language/locale picker slide
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS OnboardingPage.swift (ios/.../App/Onboarding/OnboardingPage.swift:3) defines only [welcome, record, transcribe, permission, voiceSetup] — no .languages case exists. iOS OnboardingView.swift iterates OnboardingPage.allCases and therefore never renders a languages slide. The macOS slide (macos/.../OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift) depends on four types that exist only under macos/: DictationManager (line 12, @EnvironmentObject; not in shared kit or ios/), DictationLanguageStore (line 9, ObservableObject; defined only in macos/.../DictationLanguageStore.swift alongside DictationLanguageCatalog), and LanguagePickerView (line 34; defined only in macos/.../LanguagePickerView.swift). Additionally, line 46 uses Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor) — an AppKit symbol unavailable on iOS. The LanguageManager type is in the shared kit (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift) and is portable, but it is not wired into the iOS app (no reference in ios/.../WaiComputerApp.swift or ContentView.swift). Porting requires: (1) moving DictationLanguageStore + DictationLanguageCatalog to the shared kit, (2) moving or rebuilding LanguagePickerView for iOS/UIKit, (3) replacing NSColor.windowBackgroundColor with a cross-platform equivalent, (4) wiring LanguageManager and DictationLanguageStore as environment objects in the iOS app, and (5) adding a .languages case to the iOS OnboardingPage enum.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSColor (NSColor.windowBackgroundColor at OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:46 — AppKit-only, unavailable on iOS)
DictationManager (OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:12,37 — macOS-only class, not in shared kit, not present in ios/)
DictationLanguageStore (OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:9 — defined only in macos/.../DictationLanguageStore.swift, not in shared kit)
DictationLanguageCatalog (macos/.../DictationLanguageStore.swift — macOS-only, not in shared kit)
LanguagePickerView (macos/.../LanguagePickerView.swift — macOS-only, not in shared kit or ios/)

gaps.name: Onboarding: page-progress resume across app restarts
gaps.risk: Low — small persistence addition; must handle edge case where stored index is out of range

verdict.capabilityName: Onboarding: page-progress resume across app restarts
verdict.reason: Gap is confirmed. iOS OnboardingView.swift:6 hard-codes `@State private var currentPage: Int = 0` with no UserDefaults read on init and no persistence on change. By contrast, macOS OnboardingView.swift:37 initialises `_currentPage` via `Self.initialCurrentPage(for:pages:)` (lines 282-298), which reads `UserDefaults.standard.integer(forKey: phase.currentPageKey)`, and line 64 calls `persistCurrentPage()` on every `onChange(of: currentPage)`. Neither the shared kit nor any other iOS file supplies this behaviour. The macOS persistence logic uses only `UserDefaults.standard` (Foundation, fully available on iOS) plus `UserDefaults.integer(forKey:)` / `.set(_:forKey:)` — no macOS-only symbols are involved in the persistence path. The `Carbon` import in the macOS file is used elsewhere (hotkey registration) and is irrelevant to this feature. The fix is a straightforward iOS SwiftUI + Foundation port: introduce a `currentPageKey` string constant on `AppState` (or inline it), read it in the iOS `OnboardingView.init`, and write back in `.onChange(of: currentPage)`.

gaps.name: In-app permission banner: microphone (non-blocking toast overlay)
gaps.risk: Low — isolated overlay; mic permission polling is well-understood on iOS

verdict.capabilityName: In-app permission banner: microphone (non-blocking toast overlay)
verdict.reason: Gap is confirmed. iOS AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift) has no missingPermissions, visiblePermissionBanner, refreshPermissionStatus, or permission polling timer. iOS ContentView/MainTabView (ContentView.swift:39-113) has no PermissionBanner overlay. The macOS PermissionBanner.swift handles two kinds: .microphone and .accessibility. The .microphone case uses only AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus/requestAccess, which are available on iOS via AVFoundation. The .accessibility kind (AXIsProcessTrusted at PermissionBanner.swift:118, ApplicationServices import at line 3, and MacInputPermission/MacPrivacySettings calls in WaiComputerMacApp.swift:1330-1338) is entirely macOS-specific and not applicable to iOS. Porting the microphone-only banner to iOS requires: (1) stripping the .accessibility kind and the ApplicationServices import, (2) adding missingPermissions/visiblePermissionBanner/dismissPermissionBanner/refreshPermissionStatus to iOS AppState with AVFoundation-only logic, and (3) adding the .overlay(alignment: .bottom) to MainTabView. All of this is achievable with SwiftUI and the shared kit, with no blocking iOS-unavailable dependency for the microphone case.
hiddenPlatformDeps: AXIsProcessTrusted() (PermissionBanner.swift:118 — macOS Accessibility API, no iOS equivalent)
MacPrivacySettings.openAccessibility() (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:1338 — opens macOS System Settings Privacy pane)

gaps.name: Appearance mode preference (light/dark/system) wired to scene
gaps.risk: Low — one-line modifier addition; must verify SettingsView exposes the toggle on iOS

verdict.capabilityName: Appearance mode preference (light/dark/system) wired to scene
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. macOS wires the preference in WaiComputerMacApp.swift:24 (@AppStorage read of MacThemePreferences.appearanceKey) and applies it at line 101 (.preferredColorScheme(selectedAppearanceMode.preferredColorScheme)) on the WindowGroup body. The iOS WindowGroup in WaiComputerApp.swift:208-219 has no preferredColorScheme modifier and no AppearanceMode or ThemePreferences type anywhere in the iOS target. The macOS implementation type MacAppearanceMode (DesignSystem.swift:59-73) derives preferredColorScheme via a pure SwiftUI switch returning ColorScheme? — no AppKit, NSWindow, NSPanel, or any other macOS-only symbol is involved in the appearance-to-scene wiring path. The accent side (MacAccentChoice) does use Color(nsColor:) for color resolution and would need a UIColor-based equivalent on iOS, but the core preferredColorScheme wiring is fully portable. The fix requires only: defining an equivalent AppearanceMode enum (or reusing a shared one), adding @AppStorage to the iOS App struct, and calling .preferredColorScheme(...) on the iOS WindowGroup body — all pure SwiftUI iOS 13+ APIs.

gaps.name: Accent color preference wired to scene tint
gaps.risk: Low — one-line modifier addition

verdict.capabilityName: Accent color preference wired to scene tint
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS WaiComputerApp.swift (lines 208-219) applies no .tint() modifier to the WindowGroup and has no @AppStorage accent preference key. The iOS Palette.accent is a hardcoded Color(red:green:blue:) constant, not user-selectable. The macOS implementation is NOT directly portable as-is because MacAccentChoice.tintColor and .color (DesignSystem.swift:86-111) are built entirely with Color(nsColor:) — an AppKit-only initializer unavailable on iOS. Every case including .system (NSColor.controlAccentColor) and all named colors (NSColor.systemOrange, systemBlue, etc.) use this AppKit path. NSColor.controlAccentColor has no iOS equivalent. While .tint() and @AppStorage themselves work on iOS, the color construction layer must be rewritten per platform before this can land, making this PLATFORM_LIMITED rather than straightforwardly PORTABLE.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSColor (Color(nsColor:) initializer used for all MacAccentChoice cases)
NSColor.controlAccentColor (system accent — AppKit-only, no UIColor equivalent)

gaps.name: scenePhase .active handler: dictation refresh + pending sync resume
gaps.risk: Low — depends on permission banner gap being implemented first

verdict.capabilityName: scenePhase .active handler: dictation refresh + pending sync resume
verdict.reason: The gap is real but not portable. iOS (WaiComputerApp.swift:212-216) already calls resumePendingRecordingSyncIfNeeded() on scenePhase .active — that half is parity. The missing half is the permission refresh, which consists of two macOS-only calls: (1) dictationManager.refreshPermissionState() (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:115), which delegates entirely to GlobalHotkeyManager.refreshAfterPermissionChange() + applyHotkeyAvailability() — GlobalHotkeyManager.swift imports Carbon, CGEventTap, CGEvent.post, NSWorkspace, ApplicationServices, and IOKit.hid, none of which exist on iOS; (2) appState.refreshPermissionStatus(rearmDismissed:true) (MacContentView.swift:32), which at line 1252 of WaiComputerMacApp.swift calls MacInputPermission.postEventStatus() — a check for the macOS Accessibility/TCC permission (AXIsProcessTrusted) required for text insertion via CGEvent.post. iOS has no global hotkey system, no Accessibility TCC permission for input monitoring, and no NSWorkspace. The microphone permission on iOS is handled at recording start via AVAudioSession and during onboarding; there is no equivalent runtime re-poll needed or possible on scene activation. Classifying as PLATFORM_LIMITED: the sync-resume sub-call is already present on iOS; the permission-refresh sub-call is macOS-only by design and has no meaningful iOS counterpart to port.

gaps.name: Auth view: app icon / brand image display
gaps.risk: Low — cosmetic; needs asset catalog lookup for app icon on iOS

verdict.capabilityName: Auth view: app icon / brand image display
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS AuthView.swift:17 shows Image(systemName: "brain.head.profile") — an SF Symbol placeholder — while macOS MacContentView.swift:1508 shows the actual app icon via Image(nsImage: NSApp.applicationIconImage). The subtitle at iOS:25 is hardcoded English "Your AI Second Brain"; macOS:1515 uses t("YOUR SECOND BRAIN", "ТВОЙ ВТОРОЙ МОЗГ") with LanguageManager. However, the macOS NSApp.applicationIconImage path is NOT proposed for iOS and introduces no blocking dependency on iOS. The correct iOS fix is Image("BrandIcon"), which is already present in the iOS asset catalog (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Assets.xcassets/BrandIcon.imageset/ with 1x+2x PNGs) and already used on iOS in OnboardingSlide.swift:51. The fix is entirely SwiftUI + named asset — no AppKit symbol needed. confirmedPortable=true because the branded image asset already exists in the iOS bundle; only AuthView needs to swap the systemName for Image("BrandIcon") and adopt the t()-style localized subtitle.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSApp.applicationIconImage (AppKit/macOS-only — used in macOS implementation but must NOT be ported to iOS; iOS fix uses Image("BrandIcon") named asset instead)

gaps.name: Auth view: WaiTabBar / segmented auth mode picker (Login/Register/MagicLink)
gaps.risk: Low — UI-only once magic link logic is added

verdict.capabilityName: Auth view: WaiTabBar / segmented auth mode picker (Login/Register/MagicLink)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed at multiple code levels. macOS MacContentView.swift:1485-1527 defines a local AuthMode enum with .login/.register/.magicLink and renders all three via WaiTabBar. iOS AuthView.swift:32-35 has only a two-segment Picker driven by Bool isLoginMode — no third tab, no AuthMode enum, no magicLinkSent state, and no call to requestMagicLink anywhere in the iOS target (grep returns zero hits). WaiTabBar is defined in macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift:374-404 as pure SwiftUI (Button, Text, Capsule, HStack, Binding, ForEach) — no macOS-only imports; its file imports only SwiftUI and WaiComputerKit. The shared kit already exposes APIClient.requestMagicLink() at shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:551, available to the iOS target which already imports WaiComputerKit. iOS AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift:224) lacks both magicLinkSent: Bool and any requestMagicLink wrapper. Porting requires: copy WaiTabBar into iOS DesignSystem (pure SwiftUI), add AuthMode enum, add @Published var magicLinkSent to iOS AppState, and wire apiClient.requestMagicLink() — no blocking platform dependency anywhere in the chain.

gaps.name: Library error inline banner (auto-dismissed after 6s)
gaps.risk: Low — isolated to LibraryView; out of scope for strict app shell domain but triggered from main layout

verdict.capabilityName: Library error inline banner (auto-dismissed after 6s)
verdict.reason: The iOS implementation is complete and equivalent to macOS. ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Library/LibraryView.swift lines 41–49 render a private InlineLibraryBanner view (defined at lines 555–585) via .overlay(alignment: .top) whenever viewModel.error is non-nil. Lines 164–175 implement the identical 6-second auto-dismiss using .onChange(of: viewModel.error) and Task.sleep(for: .seconds(6)), with cancellation on .onDisappear (line 177). The state variable errorAutoDismissTask is declared at line 9. This mirrors the macOS pattern in MacContentView.swift lines 527–538 exactly. The first-pass claim that iOS has no equivalent inline error banner or auto-dismiss timer is incorrect — the feature ships in full on iOS using pure SwiftUI with no macOS-only symbols.

gaps.name: Sidebar navigation: Trash section (restore / permanent delete)
gaps.risk: Medium — touches library data model; must confirm backend pagination handles trashed filter

verdict.capabilityName: Sidebar navigation: Trash section (restore / permanent delete)
verdict.reason: The first-pass claim that iOS has no Trash section is incorrect. iOS LibraryView.swift (lines 254-268) has an explicit "Trash section" with a NavigationLink to TrashView. TrashView (lines 458-551) renders all trashed recordings, exposes swipe-leading Restore and swipe-trailing Delete Permanently actions per row, and has an "Empty Trash" toolbar button with a confirmation dialog. RecordingDetailView.swift (lines 83-144) fully supports isTrash mode with Restore and Delete Permanently buttons. The view model (lines 645, 683) loads trashedRecordings from the API. Feature is fully implemented on iOS with pure SwiftUI and no platform-specific symbols.

gaps.name: Folder management: create folder from sidebar
gaps.risk: Low — well-defined API; needs sheet UI

verdict.capabilityName: Folder management: create folder from sidebar
verdict.reason: The iOS LibraryView already fully implements "New Folder" creation. At line 64-65 a "New Folder" button lives inside the ellipsis.circle toolbar Menu; lines 111-124 show the .alert("New Folder") sheet with a TextField and a Create button that calls viewModel.createFolder(name:apiClient:); lines 738-746 implement createFolder() in LibraryViewModel — identical logic to macOS. The affordance is placed in a toolbar menu rather than a sidebar button, which is the idiomatic iOS pattern for the same action. No platform-specific macOS symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, CATap, etc.) are involved. The first-pass classification of iosState=absent is incorrect; this is full parity.

perDomain.title: Onboarding flow
perDomain.summary:
macOS onboarding is a 7-slide, two-phase (pre-auth / post-auth) flow with rich permission handling, bilingual (EN/RU) content, dictation language selection, hotkey picking, a live dictation sandbox, and voice enrollment. iOS onboarding is a single-phase 5-slide flow (welcome, record feature, transcribe feature, permission, voice setup) with basic mic-only permission handling, English-only content, and no language/hotkey/sandbox slides.

Seven gaps are portable and actionable without any platform-exotic API: (1) inline app-language picker on welcome slide — needs LanguageManager wired into iOS; (2) Settings redirect for already-denied microphone; (3) 1 Hz permission polling to auto-advance; (4) dictation language picker slide — needs DictationLanguageStore ported; (5) two-phase pre/post-auth flow split; (6) current page persistence across restarts; and (7) three small VoiceSetupSlide gaps (min-duration guard, permission gate on record button, bilingual labels). Three capabilities are correct-by-design platform-specific absences: global hotkey picker, Accessibility TCC row, and System Audio TCC row. The dictation sandbox slide is also platform-limited (depends on macOS-only push-to-talk DictationManager). LanguageManager wiring is the critical dependency that unlocks the app-language picker, bilingual onboarding strings, and the languages slide in one effort.

gaps.name: Inline app language picker on welcome slide (EN / RU toggle)
gaps.risk: iOS currently has no LanguageManager injection at all; wiring it into AppState and environmentObject chain needs care.

verdict.capabilityName: Inline app language picker on welcome slide (EN / RU toggle)
verdict.reason:
The gap is real and confirmed. iOS OnboardingSlide.swift (line 1-73) handles the welcome case via OnboardingPage.content, which returns hardcoded English-only strings with no LanguageManager involvement. iOS WaiComputerApp.swift never injects LanguageManager into the SwiftUI environment and has no WAIDownloadRegion handling. OnboardingL10n lives only in the macOS target (macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingL10n.swift), not in the shared kit.

The macOS OnboardingWelcomeSlide.swift has three AppKit symbols that cannot be used on iOS verbatim: `import AppKit` (line 2), `NSApp.applicationIconImage` (line 13), and `Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor)` (line 107). These are isolated to the icon display and button background — not to the language picker logic itself.

The picker logic (lines 52-112 of OnboardingWelcomeSlide.swift) is pure SwiftUI: a VStack with HStack segmented buttons calling `languageManager.setLanguage()`. LanguageManager itself (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift) is pure Foundation/SwiftUI and iOS-compatible. iOS already imports WaiComputerKit in WaiComputerApp.swift.

Porting requires: (1) move or duplicate OnboardingL10n into the shared kit or iOS target — it is pure Foundation with no AppKit symbols; (2) inject LanguageManager.shared as @EnvironmentObject in WaiComputerApp.WindowGroup; (3) create an iOS-specific welcome slide replacing NSApp.applicationIconImage with Image("BrandIcon") (already used in OnboardingSlide.swift line 51) and Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor) with Color(.systemBackground). All substitutions are SwiftUI-standard and available on iOS 16+.

gaps.name: Value-props slide — two-card Dictate vs Record feature showcase
gaps.risk: Low — mostly content/layout change; narrow phone screen may need single-column stacking.

verdict.capabilityName: Value-props slide — two-card Dictate vs Record feature showcase
verdict.reason: The gap is real: iOS has no two-up card slide combining Dictate and Record. macOS OnboardingValuePropsSlide.swift (lines 21–40) renders an HStack of two valueCard views — one for Dictate (keyboard.badge.eye) and one for Record (waveform) — on a single screen. iOS OnboardingPage.swift splits these into two separate full-screen slides (.record at lines 31–38, .transcribe at lines 39–46) and entirely omits any Dictate card, because iOS has no global-hotkey dictation feature. This is not an accidental omission — the iOS onboarding flow (OnboardingView.swift iterates OnboardingPage.allCases, line 10) reflects a deliberate product decision: Dictate requires AX text insertion at cursor via a global hotkey, which is macOS-only. Porting the two-card layout to iOS would misrepresent iOS capabilities. Revised classification is DIFFERENT_BY_DESIGN rather than PORTABLE. The one concrete macOS-only symbol found in the implementation is NSColor.windowBackgroundColor at OnboardingValuePropsSlide.swift:83 (used for card background fill), which would need substitution with UIColor.secondarySystemBackground on iOS, but this is a trivial swap and not the blocking reason — the blocking reason is that the Dictate feature itself does not exist on iOS.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSColor.windowBackgroundColor (OnboardingValuePropsSlide.swift:83) — macOS-only; iOS equivalent is UIColor.secondarySystemBackground

gaps.name: Permission slide — microphone request with status feedback
gaps.risk: Low — standard iOS permission pattern; no platform-exotic APIs needed.

verdict.capabilityName: Permission slide — microphone request with status feedback
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon (import at macOS OnboardingView.swift:3 — Carbon.framework is macOS-only)
MacInputPermission (macOS-only type, wraps TCC status queries)
MacPrivacySettings.openMicrophone() / openAccessibility() / openSystemAudio() (macOS-only, uses x-apple.systempreferences: URL scheme)
AVCaptureDevice.requestAccess(for:) (used as canonical macOS API; iOS should keep AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission which is already in use)
verdict.reason: Gap confirmed. iOS OnboardingView.swift:158-167 discards the Bool result of AudioManager.shared.requestPermission() (_ = await ...), sets only permissionRequested=true, and delegates rendering to the generic static OnboardingSlide — no dynamic status indicator, no denied/granted branching, no Settings redirect. The macOS implementation (OnboardingView.swift:430-458) fully branches on the granted result, opens System Settings + reveals app in Finder on denial, tracks triggeredOpenMicrophoneSettings, polls on scenePhase, and feeds a rich OnboardingPermissionSlide with PermissionRow status rows and a restart affordance. The macOS-specific wrappers (MacInputPermission, MacPrivacySettings, Carbon, NSColor.windowBackgroundColor, revealAppInFinder, restartForPermissionRefresh) are all macOS-only and must not be ported directly. However, the portable core — branching on the Bool return, showing a status label (granted/denied), and offering UIApplication.shared.open(URL(string: UIApplication.openSettingsURLString)!) on denial — is pure SwiftUI + UIKit available on iOS. AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission already used in AudioManager is the correct iOS API. The fix touches only ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift and requires no shared-kit changes.

gaps.name: Permission slide — Settings-open redirect for already-denied microphone
gaps.risk: None — this is a textbook iOS permission UX pattern.

verdict.capabilityName: Permission slide — Settings-open redirect for already-denied microphone
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. On macOS (OnboardingView.swift:448-458), after AVCaptureDevice.requestAccess returns false, the code calls MacInputPermission.revealAppInFinder() and MacPrivacySettings.openMicrophone() to direct the user to System Preferences. On iOS (OnboardingView.swift:158-167), the result of AudioManager.shared.requestPermission() is entirely discarded with _ =; there is no denied-path branch at all. A full grep of every iOS Swift file finds zero calls to UIApplication.shared.open, openSettingsURLString, or any privacy URL scheme, confirming no Settings redirect exists anywhere in the iOS onboarding. The feature IS portable: the iOS equivalent of the macOS NSWorkspace.shared.open(x-apple.systempreferences:...) call is UIApplication.shared.open(URL(string: UIApplication.openSettingsURLString)!), which is pure SwiftUI-compatible UIKit available in the shared kit's iOS target. The Finder-reveal step (NSWorkspace.shared.activateFileViewerSelecting) has no iOS equivalent and would simply be omitted on iOS — it is a macOS-specific recovery aid that does not block the core Settings-redirect behaviour from being ported. The primary button title logic on iOS (primaryButtonTitle, lines 129-133) already has a permissionRequested branch, so the hook point for a "Open Settings" label in the denied state also exists and just needs to be wired up.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace.shared.activateFileViewerSelecting (Finder reveal — macOS only, no iOS equivalent, must be omitted)
NSWorkspace.shared.open (used in MacPrivacySettings.openPrivacyPane to open x-apple.systempreferences: URL — macOS only; iOS uses UIApplication.shared.open with openSettingsURLString instead)

gaps.name: Permission slide — 1 Hz polling to auto-advance once permissions become granted
gaps.risk: Minor — need to invalidate the timer on disappear to avoid leaks.

verdict.capabilityName: Permission slide — 1 Hz polling to auto-advance once permissions become granted
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. macOS OnboardingView (line 17) declares `@State private var permissionPollTimer: Timer?`, starts a 1 Hz repeating timer in `startPermissionPolling()` (lines 560–567) called from both `onAppear` (line 61) and the `scenePhase == .active` watcher (lines 67–71), and stops it inside `refreshPermissions()` when `dictationPermissionsReady` is true (lines 425–427). iOS OnboardingView has no equivalent: `permissionRequested` (line 7) is set to `true` exactly once after the one-shot `await AudioManager.shared.requestPermission()` returns (line 163) and is never re-read from the system. There is no scenePhase observer, no Timer, and no auto-advance path — the user must manually tap the primary button again after granting permission. The polling mechanism uses only cross-platform symbols: `Timer.scheduledTimer`, `@Environment(\.scenePhase)`, `AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus(for: .audio)`, and `DispatchQueue.main.async` — all available in iOS SwiftUI. The iOS-side `dictationPermissionsReady` equivalent would only need to check microphone (iOS has no accessibility or system-audio permission gates in onboarding), so none of the macOS-specific types (`MacInputPermission`, `MacPrivacySettings`, `MacAppState`, `Carbon`) enter the portable polling loop.

gaps.name: Dictation language picker slide (STT language selection)
gaps.risk: Medium — DictationLanguageStore and LanguagePickerView must be ported or a new iOS-idiomatic equivalent built; also needs wiring in Settings for post-onboarding access.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation language picker slide (STT language selection) in onboarding
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS OnboardingPage.allCases (ios/.../OnboardingPage.swift:3-10) has no .languages case and OnboardingView.swift has no branch for a language-picker slide. iOS SettingsView.swift (line 212) still uses the legacy @AppStorage("transcriptionLanguage") Picker rather than DictationLanguageStore. DictationLanguageStore and DictationLanguageStore are macOS-only files with no iOS counterpart found anywhere in ios/ or shared/WaiComputerKit/. However, the gap is portable: DictationLanguageStore (macos/.../DictationLanguageStore.swift) uses only Foundation, UserDefaults, and os.Logger — zero iOS-blocking APIs. LanguagePickerView (macos/.../LanguagePickerView.swift) uses only SwiftUI, WaiComputerKit.LanguageManager (which is already in the shared kit at shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift and imported by iOS), plus the iOS-present Palette/Typography/Spacing design tokens. The one macOS-only symbol is in the slide wrapper: OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift line 46 uses Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor) — a one-line fix to Color(uiColor: .systemBackground). The DictationManager.prefetchSessionConfigForCurrentLanguage call (slide line 37-38, DictationManager.swift:275) references a macOS-only manager, but this is an optional prefetch optimization that can be omitted on iOS without breaking core functionality. OnboardingL10n is macOS-local but is a trivial L10n helper iOS would need to add. All core logic (store, catalog, picker view) is free of platform-specific frameworks and can land on iOS with SwiftUI and the shared kit.
hiddenPlatformDeps: DictationManager.prefetchSessionConfigForCurrentLanguage (OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:37-38) — DictationManager is macOS-only; call must be dropped or replaced on iOS
OnboardingL10n (used in LanguagePickerView.swift:145 and OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:63) — defined only in macos/.../OnboardingL10n.swift, not present in iOS or shared kit

gaps.name: Two-phase (pre-auth / post-auth) onboarding flow
gaps.risk: Medium — requires splitting AppState onboarding state, updating ContentView routing, and migrating UserDefaults keys without breaking existing installs.

verdict.capabilityName: Two-phase (pre-auth / post-auth) onboarding flow
verdict.reason: The two-phase split on macOS exists entirely because of macOS-exclusive capabilities, not as a generic UX pattern that iOS is missing. The preAuth phase includes the `.hotkey` page, which configures a global push-to-talk key via GlobalHotkeyManager (NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents + CGEvent.post + Accessibility TCC). The postAuth phase includes the `.sandbox` page — a live dictation sandbox that exercises DictationManager's hotkey-driven text insertion via the same Accessibility permission. macOS OnboardingView.swift:3 imports Carbon directly; lines 419, 454, 462-463, 474, 479, 551 reference MacInputPermission.accessibilityStatus(), GlobalHotkeyManager.requestAccessibilityPermission(), CGEvent.post, MacPrivacySettings.openAccessibility/openMicrophone/openSystemAudio/restartForPermissionRefresh, and SystemAudioGate.isSupported — none of which exist on iOS. iOS has no global hotkey, no Accessibility TCC grant, no system audio capture, and no dictation text-insertion flow to sandbox. The iOS onboarding (WaiComputerApp.swift:230, OnboardingView.swift) is already a complete, appropriate single-phase flow (welcome, record, transcribe, permission, voiceSetup) that covers everything iOS can offer. There is no missing iOS capability here — the macOS two-phase structure is a direct consequence of macOS-only platform features.
hiddenPlatformDeps: CGEvent.post (text insertion governed by Accessibility TCC, cited at OnboardingView.swift:462-463)
DictationManager (global hotkey + text insertion via Accessibility, used by .hotkey and .sandbox slides)

gaps.name: Current page persistence across app restarts (resume mid-flow)
gaps.risk: Low — straightforward UserDefaults read/write; must clear the key on completeOnboarding().

verdict.capabilityName: Current page persistence across app restarts (resume mid-flow)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. macOS OnboardingView initializes currentPage from UserDefaults via initialCurrentPage(for:pages:) at OnboardingView.swift:37 and persists it on .onAppear (line 59) and .onChangeCompat(of: currentPage) (line 64) via persistCurrentPage() (lines 304-309). iOS OnboardingView (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:6) unconditionally sets @State private var currentPage: Int = 0 with no UserDefaults read on init, no .onAppear write, and no persistence in onChange (line 55 only resets permissionRequested). The shared kit has no OnboardingPhase type, no currentPageKey, and no page-persistence helpers. The macOS persistence path (lines 282-309) uses only UserDefaults.standard and Int — both are standard Foundation APIs available on iOS. The OnboardingPhase enum and its currentPageKey computed property (macos/.../OnboardingPage.swift:35-56) likewise use only Foundation. No macOS-exclusive symbol (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, etc.) appears in the persistence code path. The fix requires adding an init that reads UserDefaults, a persistCurrentPage() call in .onAppear and .onChange(of: currentPage), and an iOS-side OnboardingPhase/key constant — all portable SwiftUI + Foundation work.

gaps.name: Breadcrumb step indicator (text labels with chevrons)
gaps.risk: Low — layout only; needs breadcrumbLabel() added to iOS OnboardingPage.

verdict.capabilityName: Breadcrumb step indicator (text labels with chevrons)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed by direct code inspection. macOS OnboardingView.swift:142-164 renders an HStack of uppercased Text step labels separated by chevron.right images, with a 1.5pt Rectangle underline accent on the current page, driven by OnboardingPage.breadcrumbLabel(language:) (macos/.../OnboardingPage.swift:15-32). iOS OnboardingView.swift:71-80 renders only Capsule dot indicators (wider capsule for active, 6pt dot for inactive) with no labels or chevrons. The iOS OnboardingPage.swift has no breadcrumbLabel() method at all, and its cases differ (welcome/record/transcribe/permission/voiceSetup vs the macOS set). The pageIndicator implementation on macOS uses exclusively pure SwiftUI (HStack, VStack, Text, Image(systemName:), Rectangle, .animation) plus Palette color constants and LanguageManager — all of which are available on iOS. None of the macOS-only symbols (NSColor, Carbon, MacInputPermission, MacAppState, DictationManager, GlobalHotkeyManager, MacPrivacySettings, CGEvent, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap) appear within the pageIndicator computed property. Porting requires two steps: add breadcrumbLabel(language:) to the iOS OnboardingPage enum, and replace the capsule-dot pageIndicator body in the iOS OnboardingView with the label+chevron layout.

gaps.name: Voice setup slide — minimum 5s duration guard before submit
gaps.risk: None — additive guard only.

verdict.capabilityName: Voice setup slide — minimum 5s duration guard before submit
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed by direct reading of both files. macOS VoiceEnrollmentRecorder (macos/.../OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift:228-232) defines minDurationSeconds=5.0 and hasMinimumDuration; submit() (lines 147-153) guards on it and surfaces an error; the button is double-disabled via WaiPrimaryButtonStyle(isDisabled:) and .disabled() at lines 122-123; statusLabel shows a "Minimum is 5s." message at lines 189-193. iOS VoiceEnrollmentRecorder (ios/.../OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift:165-285) has none of these: no minDurationSeconds, no hasMinimumDuration property, submit() (lines 125-144) has no duration guard, the button is disabled only for .uploading state (line 63), and statusLabel never mentions the minimum. The shared kit (shared/WaiComputerKit/) has no VoiceEnrollmentRecorder or hasMinimumDuration at all — both platforms own their own local recorder class. The macOS implementation uses only AVFoundation, SwiftUI, WaiComputerKit, Timer, AVAudioRecorder, and standard Swift — no macOS-only frameworks. The fix is purely additive to the iOS-local VoiceEnrollmentRecorder and its view layer.

gaps.name: Voice setup slide — hasMicrophonePermission gate on record button
gaps.risk: None — permission check is one line.

verdict.capabilityName: Voice setup slide — hasMicrophonePermission gate on record button
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed by direct code inspection. macOS OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift:10 declares `let hasMicrophonePermission: Bool` and uses it at line 109 (`.disabled(!hasMicrophonePermission || recorder.state == .uploading)`) and in `statusLabel` (line 180, returns "Grant microphone access first" when false). iOS OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift has no such parameter; its recordButton at line 114 only checks `.disabled(recorder.state == .uploading)`, and its `statusLabel` has no permission-denied branch. The iOS onboarding order places `.permission` before `.voiceSetup` (OnboardingPage.swift:7-8), but the TabView uses `.page` swipe style with no navigation guard — a user who denies or skips permission can freely swipe to voiceSetup and tap the mic button, which then calls `recorder.record()` and silently fails with a generic error message. The macOS implementation relies solely on `AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus(for: .audio)` (macos/OnboardingView.swift:345), which is a fully cross-platform AVFoundation API available on iOS with identical semantics. No platform-exclusive symbols are involved. The fix is straightforward: add `hasMicrophonePermission: Bool` to iOS OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide (mirroring the macOS signature), compute it in iOS OnboardingView from AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus, wire it into the .disabled modifier and statusLabel — pure SwiftUI + AVFoundation, no iOS-unavailable dependency.

gaps.name: Voice setup slide — localized EN/RU prompt text and status labels
gaps.risk: Dependent on LanguageManager being injected into iOS first.

verdict.capabilityName: Voice setup slide — localized EN/RU prompt text and status labels
verdict.reason: The iOS file at ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift uses hardcoded English strings throughout: the section label "VOICE" (line 20), headline "Teach Wai your voice" (line 25), body copy (line 30), the promptText static string (lines 13–14), all four statusLabel branches ("Press the mic to start", "Recording…", "Recorded … Re-record or submit.", "Uploading voice signature…" — lines 154–161), all three footer buttons ("Skip for now", "Re-record", "Use this take" — lines 55–64), and the privacy disclaimer (line 68). No LanguageManager is injected or used anywhere in this file. By contrast the macOS file at macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide.swift injects LanguageManager as an @EnvironmentObject (line 15), defines a local t() helper wrapping OnboardingL10n.text(_:_:language:) (lines 204–206), and wraps every user-visible string in t(english, russian). The gap is confirmed: the iOS slide is English-only. Portability is unblocked: LanguageManager lives in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift (which iOS already imports via import WaiComputerKit at line 3 of the iOS file), OnboardingL10n would need to be either moved to the shared kit or duplicated in the iOS target, and neither AVAudioRecorder nor AVFoundation nor any SwiftUI API used here is iOS-unavailable. No macOS-exclusive symbols appear anywhere in the macOS implementation of this slide (no NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, AX, menu bar, dock, or global hotkey).

gaps.name: onboardingMicAcknowledged flag written at onboarding completion
gaps.risk: None — purely additive.

verdict.capabilityName: onboardingMicAcknowledged flag written at onboarding completion
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. macOS OnboardingView.swift:255 writes UserDefaults.standard.set(hasMicrophonePermission, forKey: MacAppState.onboardingMicAcknowledgedKey) inside completeOnboarding(), where hasMicrophonePermission derives from AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus(for: .audio). iOS WaiComputerApp.swift:302-304 completeOnboarding() writes only onboardingCompletedKey; no mic-acknowledged flag is written anywhere in the iOS code or shared kit. The macOS implementation uses only AVFoundation (AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus — available on iOS) and UserDefaults; it contains no macOS-only symbols. The constant is defined in MacOnboardingDefaultsSnapshot (a macOS-only struct) but that is a structural issue, not a blocking platform dependency — the write itself is fully portable to iOS via SwiftUI + the shared kit with no blocking symbol.

perDomain.title: Library: recording list & detail
perDomain.summary: The iOS and macOS Library domains share the same shared WaiComputerKit models (Recording, RecordingDetail, Segment, Person, Summary, ActionItem) and APIClient methods, so data-layer behavior is automatically in parity. The major gaps fall into five clusters: (1) local-recovery / backup-store integration — iOS list rows never read RecordingBackupStore, the detail VM never injects local segments, and the transcript empty state has no savedLocally/processing distinction; (2) speaker-directory management — iOS SpeakerAssignSheet cannot rename, delete, or merge persons in the global directory; (3) recording-level write actions — iOS has no inline rename from the detail header and no rename entry in the list context menu, and folder rename is absent from the library list; (4) detail sharing and export — share-link creation and export (markdown/txt/srt) are macOS-only; (5) summary generation lifecycle — iOS uses the legacy synchronous generateSummary path and therefore shows no in-progress stage indicator and no failure text. Orthogonally, macOS multi-select with bulk trash/restore/delete and a bulk-operation progress banner are absent on iOS. All identified gaps are PORTABLE (no platform-restricted capability required); effort is mostly S with three M items (multi-select, export, local-recovery detail VM, and LanguageManager threading). iOS has one capability macOS lacks: an Empty Trash button and a per-segment expand/collapse tap gesture.

gaps.name: Recording list: local-recovery marker on row ("Saved locally" / "Needs attention")
gaps.risk: RecordingBackupStore may not create backup manifests on iOS if the local-recording flow is different — verify iOS writes manifests before surfacing the UI.

verdict.capabilityName: Recording list: local-recovery marker on row ("Saved locally" / "Needs attention")
verdict.reason: Gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. macOS RecordingRowView (RecordingListView.swift:99-170) receives hasLocalRecoveryBackup and hasPermanentLocalFailure booleans computed from RecordingBackupStore.manifestsByRecordingId() in MacLibraryViewModel.swift:78-95 and passes them to the shared-kit Recording.statusDisplayText(hasLocalRecoveryBackup:hasPermanentLocalFailure:) at Recording.swift:483, which emits "Saved locally" (line 496) and "Needs attention" (line 493). The iOS RecordingRow at LibraryView.swift:597 calls only the parameterless computed property recording.statusDisplayText (Recording.swift:513-515), which defaults both booleans to false, so those two strings can never appear. iOS LibraryViewModel (LibraryView.swift:668-724) never calls RecordingBackupStore.manifestsByRecordingId() and carries no localRecoveryRecordingIDs or permanentLocalFailureRecordingIDs state. No iOS-unavailable symbols exist in the macOS implementation path: RecordingBackupStore, RecordingBackupManifest, and the parameterized statusDisplayText overload all live in WaiComputerKit (shared kit). LanguageManager is also in the shared kit (WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift). The macOS-local Palette/Typography/Spacing tokens used in RecordingRowView are cosmetic; the iOS row already uses equivalent SwiftUI styles. The fix requires only adding the two Set<String> published properties to iOS LibraryViewModel, populating them from RecordingBackupStore.manifestsByRecordingId() in loadLibrary, threading the booleans into RecordingRow, and switching to the parameterized statusDisplayText call — all using APIs already available in WaiComputerKit and already used elsewhere on iOS (RecordingViewModel.swift uses RecordingBackupStore extensively).

gaps.name: Recording list: context menu — rename recording
gaps.risk: Alert-with-TextField is idiomatic on iOS for inline rename; no risk.

verdict.capabilityName: Recording list: context menu — rename recording
verdict.reason:
Gap confirmed. macOS provides rename in two places: (1) RecordingListView.swift:43-46 — context menu "Rename…" button that calls onRequestRename(recording.id), and (2) MacRecordingDetailView.swift:337-341 — double-tap on the title Text activates an inline TextField. iOS LibraryView.swift recordingContextMenu (lines 276-317) contains only "Move to Folder" and "Move to Trash"; RecordingDetailView.swift toolbar menu (lines 81-124) likewise has only move/trash — no rename path exists anywhere in the iOS codebase (confirmed by grep returning zero hits for "rename" or "Rename" in ios/).

The rename implementation is portable: MacRecordingDetailViewModel.renameRecording() (line 209) calls apiClient.updateRecording() from WaiComputerKit with no AppKit or macOS-only symbols. The detail view rename uses pure SwiftUI (TextField, @FocusState, .onSubmit, .onTapGesture). The AppKit import and NSPasteboard usage in MacRecordingDetailView.swift are exclusively in the clipboard/share helpers (lines 210-211, 275-276), not in the rename path.

The one macOS-specific helper in the rename UI is onEscapeKeyCompat (macos/.../DesignSystem.swift:333), which uses onKeyPress(.escape)/onExitCommand to cancel an in-progress edit via the Escape key. On iOS there is no hardware Escape key, so this helper is simply not needed; focus-loss via .onChangeCompat(of: titleFieldFocused) already handles commit/cancel and is itself a straightforward pattern. This is an omit-on-iOS cosmetic convenience, not a blocking platform dependency.

iOS implementation path: add a Rename alert (UIAlertController-style .alert with TextField, matching the existing pattern in LibraryView for "New Folder" at line 111) triggered from a "Rename…" button in recordingContextMenu and in RecordingDetailView's toolbar menu; call LibraryViewModel.renameRecording() backed by apiClient.updateRecording().
hiddenPlatformDeps: onEscapeKeyCompat (macos/.../DesignSystem.swift:333) — uses onExitCommand (macOS-only) / onKeyPress(.escape) (iOS17+/macOS14+); not needed on iOS where hardware Escape key is absent

gaps.name: Recording list: multi-select with bulk trash / restore / permanent delete
gaps.risk: Edit mode on iOS is a distinct UX that may need design review; bulk-API exists but partial-failure UI also needs a banner.

verdict.capabilityName: Recording list: multi-select with bulk trash / restore / permanent delete
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed by direct code inspection. macOS uses `List(recordings, selection: $selectedRecordingIds)` at RecordingListView.swift:20 with a `Set<String>` binding (MacContentView.swift:98), toolbar buttons at MacContentView.swift:258-287 that fire when the set is non-empty, a `BulkSelectionDetailView` panel at MacContentView.swift:1312-1359 for the count>1 case, and progress tracking via `MacLibraryViewModel.bulkOperation` (MacLibraryViewModel.swift:30). iOS LibraryView.swift has none of this: no multi-select state, no EditButton, no List(selection:) binding — only single-item swipe actions and context menus. One macOS-only symbol appears in the implementation: `.onDeleteCommand` at RecordingListView.swift:76 (keyboard Delete key, unavailable on iOS). This is a supplemental keyboard shortcut, not the core feature. The rest of the implementation — SwiftUI `List(selection:)` with `Set<String>`, `EditButton`/`.editMode`, bulk-loop calling existing API methods (`trashRecording`, `restoreRecording`, `permanentlyDeleteRecording` which already exist on iOS `LibraryViewModel`), and `BulkSelectionDetailView` built entirely from SwiftUI primitives — is fully portable. `LibraryBulkOperation` lives in the macOS target but is a plain struct with no platform dependencies; an identical type can be added to iOS `LibraryViewModel`. No NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Sparkle, or other platform-exclusive symbols are involved in the bulk-select path.
hiddenPlatformDeps: onDeleteCommand (RecordingListView.swift:76) — macOS-only keyboard Delete handler; skip on iOS

gaps.name: Recording list: bulk operation progress banner
gaps.risk: Depends on multi-select being implemented first.

verdict.capabilityName: Recording list: bulk operation progress banner
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS LibraryViewModel (LibraryView.swift:643) has no bulkOperation property; the LibraryBulkOperationKind enum and LibraryBulkOperation struct exist only in the macOS target (MacLibraryViewModel.swift:4–19) and are absent from shared/WaiComputerKit. verdict.capabilityName: Detail view: summary generation progress indicator (in-progress status/stage display)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and fully confirmed by code inspection. On macOS, MacRecordingDetailView.swift:534-543 reads detail.summaryGeneration, checks generationState.isActive, and calls summaryGenerationProgress(state:) which renders a ProgressView + stage text spinner (lines 686-699). MacRecordingDetailViewModel.swift:117 also uses summaryGeneration.isActive to select a 2-second polling interval, and shouldAutoRefresh (line 112 chain) returns true whenever summaryGeneration.isActive is true regardless of recording.status. On iOS, RecordingDetailViewModel.swift:73-79 (shouldAutoRefresh) only returns true for .pendingUpload/.uploading/.processing recording statuses — it never consults summaryGeneration.isActive, so polling stops once recording.status==.ready even if a summary job is still queued or running. RecordingDetailView.swift:201-204 (detailRefreshKey) is derived solely from recording.status, so the polling .task never re-fires for an active summary job on a ready recording. SummaryTabView (RecordingDetailView.swift:218-345) receives only summary: Summary? and onGenerate: ()→Void — no SummaryGenerationState is passed, and there is no ProgressView or stage text anywhere in the view. The macOS summaryGenerationProgress function uses only pure SwiftUI: ProgressView, HStack, Text, .controlSize(.small) (iOS 15+), and design tokens (Spacing, Typography, Palette) that are all already defined in ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift. No AppKit, NSPanel, NSWindow, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Sparkle, or any other macOS-only symbol is present in the progress indicator path. The fix requires: (1) passing SummaryGenerationState? to SummaryTabView, (2) adding a progress HStack with ProgressView + stage text, (3) expanding RecordingDetailViewModel.shouldAutoRefresh to also return true when summaryGeneration.isActive, and (4) including summaryGeneration status in detailRefreshKey.

perDomain.title: Live recording & import
perDomain.summary: The iOS client has solid parity on the core recording loop (mic capture, start/stop, pause/resume, local backup, background sync, WebSocket reconnection banners, import). The main gaps are: (1) PORTABLE gaps in the live view — no committed/interim transcript split, no speaker labels, no discard action, capped non-auto-scrolling transcript area; (2) mid-recording WS disconnect kills the iOS recording instead of continuing locally; (3) missing fold-at-start support, disk-full guard, upload policy guard, and cleanup race protection; (4) recording UI strings are English-only; (5) system audio (CATap/DualAudioCapture), the audio input source selector, the system audio header indicator, menu bar entry point, and macOS activity flags are all correctly PLATFORM_LIMITED or NOT_APPLICABLE and should not be ported.

The gap is real and confirmed at multiple levels.

VIEW LAYER (RecordingView.swift:85-108): iOS uses a plain ScrollView capped at maxHeight:150 with a single Text(viewModel.currentTranscript). There is no ScrollViewReader, no auto-scroll, and no split committed/interim display. macOS (LiveRecordingView.swift:63-101) has an unconstrained ScrollViewReader wrapping a LazyVStack, fires proxy.scrollTo("transcript-bottom", anchor: .bottom) via onChangeCompat on every transcript change, and renders two separate Text nodes — one for committed text (sharp) and one for interim text (faded italic).

VIEWMODEL LAYER (RecordingViewModel.swift:37-38): The iOS ViewModel declares committedTranscript and interimText as plain private stored properties, not @Published. The macOS MacRecordingViewModel (lines 86, 88) declares both as @Published var committedTranscript and @Published var interimTranscript. This means even if the iOS view tried to bind to these fields, the binding does not exist.

NO BLOCKING PLATFORM DEPENDENCIES: The macOS transcript scroll block (LiveRecordingView.swift:63-101) uses only: ScrollViewReader, ScrollView, LazyVStack, Text, onChangeCompat (defined in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Compat/OnChangeCompat.swift — cross-platform), Spacing/Typography/Palette (shared design tokens), and .id("transcript-bottom"). None of these are macOS-exclusive. The outer LiveRecordingView does import AppKit and uses MacAppState/MacRecordingViewModel/SystemAudioWarningPolicy, but those are in surrounding code, not in the transcript block itself. The iOS equivalents (AppState, RecordingViewModel) are already present.

FIX REQUIRED: (1) Promote committedTranscript and interimText to @Published in ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Recording/RecordingViewModel.swift. (2) Replace the capped ScrollView block in RecordingView.swift:85-108 with a ScrollViewReader+LazyVStack matching the macOS pattern, consuming committedTranscript and interimTranscript. No shared kit changes needed.

RecordingView.swift lines 88-95 renders a single `viewModel.currentTranscript` in plain `.body` font with no styling split. The fix requires: (1) promoting committedTranscript and adding interimTranscript as @Published in RecordingViewModel.swift, (2) updating handleWebSocketEvent to set them independently (the logic already exists — lines 779-793 already separate final vs interim paths), and (3) updating RecordingView.swift to render two Text views mirroring the macOS layout. verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. macOS MacRecordingViewModel.swift:1351-1356 handles `.disconnected` during `.recording` by calling `continueRecordingWithoutLiveTranscription()`, which sets `liveTranscriptionOffline=true` and calls `ws.stopRealtimeStreamingForLocalRecording(reason:)` — keeping the recording alive. iOS RecordingViewModel.swift:794-796 instead calls `handleStreamingFailure()`, which at line 658 immediately sets phase to `.finalizing`, stopping the recording. The iOS `liveTranscriptionOffline` property is declared identically at line 35; `webSocketManager` is the same shared `WebSocketManager` type at line 44; and `stopRealtimeStreamingForLocalRecording` is public in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/WebSocketManager.swift:330. `SentryHelper.addBreadcrumb` is also shared. No macOS-only symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Sparkle, Carbon hotkeys, Accessibility/AX text insertion) appear anywhere in the `continueRecordingWithoutLiveTranscription` call path. gaps.risk: Low — only activated when device storage is exhausted.

verdict.capabilityName: Disk-full guard in audio write loop

The @discardableResult annotation itself was added to the shared kit precisely to support both call sites, meaning the shared kit is already prepared for the iOS fix. The macOS implementation uses only platform-neutral Swift: t() localization, MainActor.run, Task.detached, and self.error — no NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Sparkle, Carbon, AX, or any other iOS-unavailable symbol. gaps.risk: Low — only affects very short accidental recordings.

RecordingAudioUploadPolicy is in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Audio/RecordingAudioUploadPolicy.swift and imports only Foundation with no platform symbols. AudioFileWriter (also shared kit) already exposes totalBytesWritten: Int64 and durationSeconds: Double, which are the exact inputs the policy needs. The only implementation wrinkle is that iOS sets audioFileWriter = nil at line 271 before the upload decision; the fix is to capture the writer's totalBytesWritten and durationSeconds before nilling it — a minor local refactor identical to the macOS pattern. No macOS-exclusive symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, SUUpdater, AXUIElement, menu bar, dock) appear anywhere in the upload policy path.

gaps.name: Cleanup task sequencing guard (prevent double-start during finalizing)
gaps.risk: Edge case fix only — low risk.

verdict.capabilityName: Cleanup task sequencing guard (prevent double-start during finalizing)
verdict.reason: The macOS guard (MacRecordingViewModel.swift:105,117,228-236,489-491) consists of two plain Swift primitives — `private var isCleaningUp = false` and `private var cleanupTask: Task<Void, Never>?` — plus the logic: if isCleaningUp, await cleanupTask?.value before allowing a new start; in stopRecording(), if already .finalizing, await cleanupTask?.value and return. Neither symbol touches NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, or any other macOS-only API. The iOS RecordingViewModel.swift (line 108) has only `guard phase == .idle else { return }` in startRecording, no isCleaningUp property, and no cleanupTask property (grep of the entire ios/ tree returns zero matches). The iOS stopRecording() (line 246) guards only `phase == .recording`, so a caller invoking startRecording while cleanup is in flight (phase == .finalizing but isCleaningUp conceptually true) slips through the idle guard and races. The fix is identical Swift Concurrency code — a Bool and a Task<Void, Never>? with await — fully portable to @MainActor iOS SwiftUI with no blocking dependency.
