MacSearchView.swift carries five .accessibilityIdentifier calls at the cited lines (38 "search-field", 63 "search-submit-button", 68 "search-bar", 101 "search-empty-state", 184 per-row via MacSearchPresentation.resultRowIdentifier). ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Search/SearchView.swift has zero .accessibilityIdentifier calls across its 176 lines. The iOS app does use the modifier elsewhere (OnboardingView, SettingsView, McpConnectView, LibraryView, ContentView), confirming the pattern is fully established and available. The .accessibilityIdentifier() modifier is a standard SwiftUI View modifier present on all Apple platforms; no macOS-exclusive symbols are involved in the identifier machinery. The macOS view references MacAppState and MacMainLayoutMetrics but those are layout/state concerns entirely separate from applying string identifiers to SwiftUI controls. Adding identifiers to SearchView.swift (the TextField, clear/submit buttons, the search HStack container, the empty-state view, and each result NavigationLink row) requires only SwiftUI and the shared kit SearchResult.recordingId — no platform-limited dependency blocks this.

gaps.name: UI test search response injection (#if DEBUG)
gaps.risk: Low; test infrastructure only, no prod impact.

verdict.capabilityName: UI test search response injection (#if DEBUG)
verdict.reason: The macOS implementation is fully confirmed at MacSearchView.swift:132-137 (the #if DEBUG guard) and WaiComputerMacApp.swift:1434-1443 (the uiTestSearchResponse method on AppState), backed by MacTestingSupport.swift which defines MacTestingMode (ProcessInfo env reads) and MacUITestFixtures (plain Codable JSON). Grep of ios/ finds zero occurrences of uiTestSearchResponse or UITestFixtures; the iOS SearchView.swift:20-24 calls viewModel.search directly from .onSubmit with no intercept path. SearchResponse is defined in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Models/SearchResult.swift:31 and is fully portable. The injection method body itself contains no macOS-only symbols — no NSWindow, Sparkle, CGEvent, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, or similar. The only dependency is ProcessInfo.processInfo.environment (available on iOS) and the shared SearchResponse type. Porting requires an iOS-side testing mode enum, an iOS fixtures enum, adding uiTestSearchResponse to the iOS AppState, and adding the #if DEBUG guard to SearchView — all achievable with SwiftUI and WaiComputerKit alone.

perDomain.title: Settings
perDomain.summary: The Settings domain has 6 capabilities at full parity (identity/voice, MCP connect, summary settings, sign out, delete account, version display), 4 iOS-only extras (privacy/terms links, storage, export, audio settings placeholders), and 12 genuine macOS-over-iOS gaps. Of those gaps: 5 are PLATFORM_LIMITED or NOT_APPLICABLE (accessibility permission row, system-audio permission/CATap, global hotkey pickers, TCC recovery actions — all blocked by iOS hardware/OS constraints); 1 is NOT_APPLICABLE (Sparkle beta/update controls); 1 is NOT_APPLICABLE (dock-icon toggle); 1 is DIFFERENT_BY_DESIGN requiring a StoreKit 2 decision (billing/subscription); and 4 are genuinely PORTABLE with no platform blocker: app-language picker (S effort — LanguageManager already in shared kit, just needs wiring into iOS root view and a settings row), Telegram connect/QR/pairing flow (M effort — all API calls are in shared kit, QR needs UIImage instead of NSImage), appearance theme + accent-color picker (M effort — needs iOS-side theme store and .preferredColorScheme() wiring), and transcription/dictation language picker alignment with DictationLanguageStore (M effort — iOS currently uses a local @AppStorage value rather than the same backend-persisted store as macOS). The microphone permission status row in Settings is also PORTABLE (S) as an iOS quality-of-life improvement. The payment-mode debug toggle is NOT_APPLICABLE since it gates a web-checkout flow prohibited on iOS App Store.

gaps.name: Account info display (email + member-since date)
gaps.risk: Minimal; date-only cosmetic change.

verdict.capabilityName: Account info display (email + member-since date)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed by reading both files. macOS MacSettingsView.swift:170-175 calls MacDateFormatting.string(from:dateStyle:timeStyle:language:) passing languageManager.current, so the member-since date respects the in-app language set via LanguageManager. iOS SettingsView.swift:50 instead uses the Foundation FormatStyle shorthand user.createdAt.formatted(date: .abbreviated, time: .omitted) which follows the system locale and ignores LanguageManager entirely — and the surrounding string "Member since ..." is hardcoded English, not a localization key. LanguageManager is in the shared WaiComputerKit (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift) and is already imported by iOS (import WaiComputerKit is at SettingsView.swift:2); iOS simply does not inject or use it in SettingsView. MacDateFormatting itself (DesignSystem.swift:161-185) uses only Foundation (DateFormatter, Locale) — no AppKit, no NSPanel, no NSWindow, no NSStatusItem, no NSWorkspace, no CGEvent, no CATap, no Sparkle, no Carbon hotkeys, no menu-bar/dock APIs. The fix is fully portable: inject LanguageManager into iOS SettingsView via @EnvironmentObject, replace the hardcoded formatted() call with a DateFormatter using languageManager.current locale (mirroring MacDateFormatting logic), and replace the hardcoded "Member since" string with a localization key.

gaps.name: App-language picker (in-session EN/RU switch via LanguageManager)
gaps.risk: Low. LanguageManager is already in the shared kit. Risk: iOS localisation bundle must ship the same en/ru .strings files as macOS.

verdict.capabilityName: App-language picker (in-session EN/RU switch via LanguageManager)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed by reading the actual source. macOS wires LanguageManager.shared at WaiComputerMacApp.swift:80 as @StateObject, injects it via .environmentObject(languageManager) at line 94, sets .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) at line 93, and exposes AppLanguagePicker inside MacSettingsView.swift:206. iOS WaiComputerApp.swift never instantiates LanguageManager, never injects it as an environment object, never sets the locale environment modifier, and ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift has no AppLanguagePicker row. AppLanguagePicker.swift imports only SwiftUI and WaiComputerKit — zero macOS-exclusive symbols (no NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, SUUpdater, or AX text-insertion APIs). LanguageManager.swift in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/ uses only Foundation, SwiftUI, and os with no platform guards. Landing on iOS requires three SwiftUI-only changes: instantiate LanguageManager.shared and pass it as .environmentObject in WaiComputerApp, set .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) on ContentView, and add AppLanguagePicker() as a row in SettingsView. No blocking platform dependency exists.

gaps.name: Dictation on/off toggle
gaps.risk: Fundamental platform constraint. A partial iOS analog (custom keyboard extension) would be a separate product feature, not a settings row.

verdict.capabilityName: Settings: Dictation on/off toggle
verdict.reason:
The gap is real and the feature is not portable to iOS. Confirmed by direct code inspection:

iOS SettingsView.swift (lines 1–185) has no DictationManager reference, no dictation section, and no on/off toggle of any kind for dictation. The iOS TranscriptionSettingsView (SettingsView.swift:216–280) does have a "dictation post-filter" toggle bound to dictationPostFilterEnabled — but this is the server-side LLM cleanup pass, not the feature-enable toggle (isFeatureEnabled). The feature-enable toggle (MacSettingsView.swift:314 — Toggle(isOn: $dictationManager.isFeatureEnabled)) is macOS-only and absent from iOS.

The macOS DictationManager.swift is itself laced with iOS-incompatible symbols: NSRunningApplication at line 158, NSWorkspace.shared.frontmostApplication at line 450, and NSSound at lines 497 and 808. GlobalHotkeyManager.swift imports Carbon and IOKit.hid at lines 2–3 and calls NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (lines 611 and 619) and AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions (line 80) — global keyboard monitoring gated by macOS Accessibility TCC, which has no equivalent on iOS. TextInserter.swift uses NSPasteboard at line 67, CGEventSource at line 149, and CGEvent.post(tap:.cgSessionEventTap) at lines 170–171 to simulate Cmd+V into other apps — an operation that is architecturally impossible on iOS. DictationOverlayPanel.swift subclasses NSPanel (line 6), a macOS-only class.

The entire feature premise — a global push-to-talk hotkey that monitors keystrokes across all apps and then pastes transcribed text into the currently focused third-party application — has no iOS equivalent due to iOS process isolation and the absence of global input monitoring and cross-app text injection APIs.
hiddenPlatformDeps: CGEventSource + CGEvent.post(tap:.cgSessionEventTap) (TextInserter.swift:149,170,171) — CGEventTap / cross-app event posting

gaps.name: Dictation push-to-talk hotkey picker
gaps.risk: Not applicable to iOS hardware.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation push-to-talk hotkey picker
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the classification is correct. The iOS SettingsView.swift has no hotkey picker, no DictationHotkey reference, and no dictationManager — confirmed by reading the full file and grepping every iOS source file. The macOS DictationHotkey enum (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413-461) is a macOS-only type: its keyCode property consumes Carbon constants (kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand — line 447-451) imported via `import Carbon` (line 2), and its requiredFlags property returns NSEvent.ModifierFlags (line 456), which is AppKit-only. The underlying GlobalHotkeyManager that the picker configures is built entirely on NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (line 611), NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents (line 631), CGEvent.post, and AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions (line 80) — none of which exist on iOS. The enum cases themselves (right_option, left_option, right_command, fn, control_option) describe physical modifier keys absent from iPhone/iPad hardware. There is no equivalent in shared/WaiComputerKit. This is not an accidental omission: iOS has no system-wide modifier-key interception API, so the feature cannot be ported without a fundamentally different interaction model (e.g., a long-press gesture, volume button, or Action button on iPhone 15 Pro+).
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon (import Carbon; kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand, kVK_Function keycodes)

gaps.name: Dictation hands-free hotkey picker (double-tap to start)
gaps.risk: Platform limitation.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation hands-free hotkey picker (double-tap to start)
verdict.reason:
The gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. The macOS Picker at MacSettingsView.swift:339-352 binds to DictationManager.selectedHandsFreeHotkey / updateHandsFreeHotkey, which resolves a DictationHotkey? from a UserDefaults string (DictationManager.swift:127-130, 293-294). The iOS SettingsView.swift has no dictation section whatsoever — no DictationManager, no hotkey picker, no hands-free concept.

The capability is not portable to iOS because it is built on an irreducible stack of macOS-only APIs. DictationHotkey is defined inside GlobalHotkeyManager.swift (line 413), which is a macOS-only file that imports Carbon, IOKit.hid, ApplicationServices, and Cocoa. The DictationHotkey.keyCode property uses kVK_RightOption, kVK_RightCommand, and kVK_Option from Carbon/Events.h (lines 447-451). DictationHotkey.requiredFlags uses NSEvent.ModifierFlags (line 456). GlobalHotkeyManager uses NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (lines 611, 619) to intercept system-wide modifier and key events — an API that does not exist on iOS. DictationManager itself depends on NSRunningApplication (line 158), NSWorkspace.shared.frontmostApplication (line 450), and DictationOverlayPanel which is an NSPanel subclass (line 154). The semantics of the feature — picking a global modifier key (RightOption, RightCommand, Fn, ControlOption) that fires dictation from any foreground app — have no iOS equivalent. iOS has no global hotkey interception, no background-accessible NSEvent stream, no modifier-key monitoring outside the focused app. The double-tap-to-start trigger is implemented via CGEventTap path / NSEvent global monitor in GlobalHotkeyManager, none of which is available on iOS.
hiddenPlatformDeps: CGEventTap (referenced in GlobalHotkeyManager comments and legacy path)
global hotkey / system-wide modifier key interception

gaps.name: Dictation post-filter (LLM cleanup before insertion) toggle
gaps.risk: Low; setting already syncs to backend.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation post-filter (LLM cleanup before insertion) toggle
verdict.reason: The iOS toggle at SettingsView.swift:242-255 exists and correctly persists the setting server-side via updateSettings (saveDictationPostFilterEnabled, line 279-281), but it has no consuming runtime on iOS. On macOS, DictationManager.swift:262 loads the flag into aiCleanupEnabled, which is read at line 744 during the dictation→cleanup→TextInserter pipeline: the flag gates a call to apiClient.cleanupDictation() (WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:1271, shared kit), and the cleaned text is then passed to TextInserter.insert() (DictationManager.swift:807). iOS has zero references to DictationManager, TextInserter, cleanupDictation, or aiCleanupEnabled — the dictation-into-apps feature does not exist on iOS at all, so the toggle controls nothing. The macOS text-insertion path (TextInserter.swift) is built entirely on iOS-unavailable APIs: NSPasteboard (clipboard write), NSRunningApplication (refocus target app), CGEventSource with combinedSessionState, CGEvent with cgSessionEventTap (synthetic Cmd+V keystrokes), CGEventFlags, and MacInputPermission (Accessibility TCC). iOS sandboxing prohibits injecting keystrokes or clipboard content into other apps entirely, making the dictation-into-apps concept non-portable. The gap is real and the blocker is architectural, not a missing SwiftUI toggle.

gaps.name: Microphone permission status row + grant/open-settings action
gaps.risk: Low. Standard iOS permission flow.

verdict.capabilityName: Microphone permission status row + grant/open-settings action in Settings
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed: iOS SettingsView (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift) has no microphone permission status row at all. Mic permission on iOS is requested only during onboarding (OnboardingView.swift:158-167) via AudioManager.requestPermission() which calls AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission(). The macOS permissionRow helper at MacSettingsView.swift:1089 is pure SwiftUI in its UI layer, but the surrounding implementation carries three iOS-unavailable symbols: (1) MacPrivacySettings.openMicrophone() / openPrivacyPane() at GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:10-47 calls NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) to open System Preferences — NSWorkspace is macOS-only; (2) MacPrivacySettings.restartForPermissionRefresh() calls NSWorkspace.shared.openApplication() and NSApp.terminate(nil) — both AppKit/macOS-only; (3) MacInputPermission.Status.staleNeedsRestart drives a distinct restart-required UI state that has no iOS equivalent and is backed by AXIsProcessTrusted() (ApplicationServices). The requestMicrophonePermission() handler (MacSettingsView.swift:1214-1239) also uses AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus/requestAccess, whereas the existing iOS code uses AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission() — a different API. The iOS equivalent of 'open settings' requires UIApplication.shared.open(URL(string: UIApplication.openSettingsURLString)!), which is absent from the macOS path entirely. The feature concept is portable but the concrete implementation is not: a new iOS-specific permission row must use AVAudioApplication, UIApplication.openSettingsURLString, and drop the staleNeedsRestart status and all NSWorkspace/NSApp calls.
hiddenPlatformDeps: import Cocoa / import Carbon / import ApplicationServices (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:1-4)
AVCaptureDevice.authorizationStatus + AVCaptureDevice.requestAccess (MacSettingsView.swift:1057,1221) — recommended API differs on iOS where AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission is used

gaps.name: Accessibility permission status row (global hotkey prereq)
gaps.risk: Not applicable.

verdict.capabilityName: Accessibility permission status row (global hotkey prereq)
verdict.reason: The first-pass classification is correct. The macOS permissionRow at MacSettingsView.swift:374-381 calls MacInputPermission.accessibilityStatus() (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:67-68), which invokes AXIsProcessTrusted() from ApplicationServices. The row exists solely to gate GlobalHotkeyManager, which uses NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents for key detection and CGEvent.post for text insertion. iOS has no equivalent of the macOS Accessibility TCC trust model, no global hotkey API, and no cross-app event injection. The iOS SettingsView.swift has no permission rows at all — only navigation links to sub-screens. The shared WaiComputerKit has zero AX/Carbon/CGEvent symbols in production code. There is no iOS counterpart to model, and none is needed: the capability the permission gates (system-wide modifier-key hotkey + CGEvent paste) simply does not exist on iOS.

gaps.name: System Audio (CATap) permission status row + test/grant action
gaps.risk: Platform-blocked.

verdict.capabilityName: System Audio (CATap) permission status row + test/grant action
verdict.reason: The macOS feature is real and correctly cited. Both SystemAudioGate.swift and SystemAudioCapture.swift in shared/WaiComputerKit are wrapped in #if os(macOS) / #endif (SystemAudioGate.swift:1,25; SystemAudioCapture.swift:1,614), so they are never compiled for iOS. The iOS SettingsView.swift has no system-audio section of any kind — confirmed by reading the full file. The capability cannot be ported to iOS because iOS provides no OS-level API to tap other processes' audio output; the entire feature depends on the macOS Core Audio Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) which is absent from iOS. This is a deliberate OS sandbox boundary, not a missing SwiftUI wrapper.
hiddenPlatformDeps: CATapDescription (macOS 14.2+ Core Audio struct; absent from iOS AudioToolbox)
AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap (macOS 14.2+ HAL API; not present on iOS)
AudioHardwareDestroyProcessTap (macOS HAL API; not present on iOS)
AudioHardwareCreateAggregateDevice / AudioHardwareDestroyAggregateDevice (macOS HAL; not present on iOS)
kAudioTapPropertyFormat (macOS HAL property selector; absent from iOS CoreAudio)
AudioDeviceCreateIOProcIDWithBlock / AudioDeviceStart / AudioDeviceStop (macOS HAL IOProc APIs; not present on iOS)

gaps.name: Dictation re-run setup / reveal in Finder / reset TCC permissions recovery actions
gaps.risk: Not applicable to iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation re-run setup / reveal in Finder / reset TCC permissions recovery actions
verdict.reason: All three recovery buttons in MacSettingsView.swift:424-450 address macOS-specific problems that have no iOS analog. (1) "Re-run setup" calls `appState.resetOnboardingForSetupRerun()` (WaiComputerMacApp.swift:810-825) — the method exists only on MacAppState, not on the shared AppState; iOS has no onboarding permission setup flow requiring recovery. (2) "Reveal in Finder" calls `NSWorkspace.shared.activateFileViewerSelecting([Bundle.main.bundleURL])` (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:100) — NSWorkspace is AppKit/macOS-only and there is no iOS equivalent for exposing the app bundle in a file picker. (3) "Reset TCC permissions + restart" calls `MacInputPermission.resetTCCEntries()` (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:214-234) which spawns `/usr/bin/tccutil reset …` via `Process()`, then `MacPrivacySettings.restartForPermissionRefresh()` (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:22-34) which uses `NSWorkspace.shared.openApplication` + `NSApp.terminate(nil)` to relaunch the app. None of these primitives exist on iOS: `Process` is unavailable, `tccutil` does not exist, `NSWorkspace`/`NSApp` are AppKit-only, and iOS apps cannot programmatically terminate themselves. iOS has no TCC.db, no accessibility permission concept analogous to macOS AX, and the OS exclusively manages microphone/permission grants via Settings.app. The gap is real (absent from iOS SettingsView.swift which shows no equivalent section) but it is absent by design — the underlying macOS-specific failure modes do not exist on iOS.

gaps.name: Billing / subscription section (plan status, usage gauge, upgrade, cancel, promo code)
gaps.risk: High. Requires StoreKit 2 integration, new backend IAP receipt validation, and App Review compliance. Cannot reuse the web-checkout BillingSection at all.

verdict.capabilityName: Billing / subscription section (plan status, usage gauge, upgrade, cancel, promo code)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS SettingsView.swift (lines 34–185) has no billing section whatsoever — no subscription status, usage gauge, upgrade button, promo code input, or cancel flow. The macOS implementation at macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Billing/BillingSection.swift contains two macOS-only symbols: `import AppKit` (line 1) and `NSWorkspace.shared.open(url)` (line 511) for opening the checkout URL. However, both are trivially replaced on iOS using SwiftUI's `@Environment(\.openURL)` environment action, which is the standard cross-platform URL-opening idiom. All billing API methods (getBillingSubscription, getBillingUsage, listBillingPlans, createBillingCheckout, claimBillingPromoCode, cancelBillingSubscription) live in the shared kit at shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Billing/BillingClient.swift and are fully available to iOS. All billing models (BillingPlan, BillingSubscription, BillingUsage, BillingDisplayPeriod, BillingDisplayRegion) are in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Billing/BillingModels.swift. The onChangeCompat helper is in the shared kit. Typography and Palette exist in ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift. MacAppState must be swapped for iOS AppState (which already exposes getAPIClient()), MacDateFormatting replaced with DateFormatter or Date.formatted(), OnboardingL10n replaced with an iOS equivalent (currently absent from iOS), and BillingCheckoutRefreshStore (macos/Features/Settings/PaymentModeToggle.swift) replaced with a simple @AppStorage key. None of these are blocking iOS-unavailable platform capabilities — they are macOS-only wrappers around portable logic. The first-pass classification of DIFFERENT_BY_DESIGN is incorrect; this is a straightforward parity gap that is portable to iOS.
hiddenPlatformDeps: MacAppState (macOS-only ObservableObject) — swap for iOS AppState
MacDateFormatting (macos/Core/DesignSystem.swift) — replace with DateFormatter or Date.formatted()
OnboardingL10n (macos/App/Onboarding/OnboardingL10n.swift) — not present in iOS; needs iOS equivalent for localized error strings
BillingCheckoutRefreshStore (macos/Features/Settings/PaymentModeToggle.swift) — macOS-only @AppStorage key holder; replace with a plain @AppStorage key on iOS

gaps.name: Billing region picker (Global vs RU for Russian UI users)
gaps.risk: Blocked by same StoreKit requirement.

verdict.capabilityName: Billing region picker (Global vs RU for Russian UI users)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and correctly classified. iOS has zero billing UI of any kind — the SettingsView (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift) contains Account, Recording, Data, Integrations, About, Logout, and Delete Account sections only; no BillingSection, no BillingDisplayRegion reference, no isRussianUI anywhere in the iOS tree. The macOS BillingSection.swift:81-83 derives isRussianUI from LanguageManager (shared kit, portable), and the regionPicker at lines 140-159 uses only SwiftUI primitives (Picker, Text, Binding, Task, AppStorage) — those symbols are iOS-compatible in isolation. However, the wider BillingSection depends on MacAppState (macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/WaiComputerMacApp.swift, macOS-only ObservableObject), and the checkout flow at line 511 uses NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) which is AppKit/macOS-only. More fundamentally, Apple App Store Review Guideline 3.1.1 prohibits directing iOS users to an external web payment page for digital goods/subscriptions — the web-billing flow that the region picker feeds into is not permissible on iOS. The absence is therefore intentional platform policy, not an oversight.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace (AppKit) — used at BillingSection.swift:511 in the checkout trigger that the region picker feeds into
MacAppState — macOS-only ObservableObject injected as @EnvironmentObject at BillingSection.swift:45; iOS uses the separate AppState type

gaps.name: Telegram integration — connect/disconnect/QR/code-entry/status
gaps.risk: Medium. QR image generation needs UIImage path instead of NSImage. Polling task lifecycle must tie to iOS view lifecycle (onDisappear). Otherwise low risk.

verdict.capabilityName: Telegram integration — connect/disconnect/QR/code-entry/status
verdict.reason:
The gap is real and fully confirmed. ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift:89-94 shows the Integrations section contains only McpConnectView(); a grep of the entire ios/ tree returns zero Telegram hits. There is no TelegramSettingsView.swift anywhere under ios/.

The macOS implementation in MacSettingsView.swift:583-709 has exactly two platform-specific symbols that block a direct copy: (1) Image(nsImage: qr) + NSImage/NSCIImageRep at lines 636/1453-1456 used to render the QR code, and (2) NSWorkspace.shared.open(deepLink) at line 1433 used to open the Telegram deep link. Both are trivially replaced on iOS: the QR pipeline (CIFilter CIQRCodeGenerator → CIImage → scaled) is identical; iOS just uses UIImage(ciImage:) instead of NSImage/NSCIImageRep; and UIApplication.shared.open() replaces NSWorkspace.shared.open(). All API methods (getTelegramLinkStatus, startTelegramLink, claimTelegramLinkCode, unlinkTelegram) and both model types (TelegramLinkStatus, TelegramPairing) are already public in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:645-664 and Models/User.swift:467-499, so the iOS view can consume them directly. The polling loop, DisclosureGroup manual-code-entry path, and all SwiftUI layout primitives are platform-neutral.

gaps.name: Appearance — theme (System/Light/Dark) + accent-color picker + live preview
gaps.risk: Medium. Shared kit needs theme preference types, and the iOS root view must apply .preferredColorScheme() + tint from stored prefs. Accent-color implementation on iOS differs (global tint vs per-component) — needs design decision.

verdict.capabilityName: Appearance — theme (System/Light/Dark) + accent-color picker + live preview
verdict.reason: The gap is real. iOS SettingsView.swift (lines 34–184) has no Appearance section and no AppearanceSettingsView.swift exists anywhere under ios/. The iOS DesignSystem.swift hard-codes a single static amber accent (Palette.accent = Color(red:0.82,green:0.49,blue:0.18)) with no user-selectable switching. The macOS implementation (MacSettingsView.swift:754–795, DesignSystem.swift:59–159) is blocked from direct reuse on iOS by Color(nsColor:) calls throughout MacAccentChoice.color/previewColor and Palette (DesignSystem.swift:91–110, 192, 201, 216) — nsColor is AppKit-only and does not compile on iOS. However, none of the blocking symbols are audio, hotkey, window-management, or OS-exclusive UI primitives: Color(nsColor:) maps 1-for-1 to Color(uiColor:) on iOS for system colors, or to plain Color literals. MacAppearanceMode.preferredColorScheme (DesignSystem.swift:66–72) uses SwiftUI ColorScheme which is fully iOS-compatible. Applying the user choice via .preferredColorScheme() and .tint() (as in WaiComputerMacApp.swift:101–102) is identical SwiftUI on iOS. A port requires: (1) a parallel accent-choice type using Color(uiColor:)/Color literals instead of Color(nsColor:), (2) an Appearance row in iOS SettingsView, (3) an AppearanceSettingsView.swift, and (4) wiring @AppStorage keys to .preferredColorScheme/.tint in the iOS app entry point. No Carbon, NSWindow, NSPanel, NSStatusItem, CATap, CGEventTap, Sparkle, or other iOS-unavailable subsystems are involved.
hiddenPlatformDeps: Color(nsColor:) — AppKit initializer used in MacAccentChoice.color/previewColor (DesignSystem.swift:91–110) and Palette (DesignSystem.swift:192,201,216); must be replaced with Color(uiColor:) or plain Color literals on iOS

gaps.name: Dictation language multi-select picker (DictationLanguageStore / LanguagePickerView)
gaps.risk: Medium. Need to verify DictationLanguageStore is in the shared kit or create an iOS equivalent. Back-end must support the same language list endpoint.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation language multi-select picker (DictationLanguageStore / LanguagePickerView)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. iOS TranscriptionSettingsView (SettingsView.swift:212) uses a bare `@AppStorage("transcriptionLanguage")` string with a single-value SwiftUI Picker — it reads/writes only the legacy key and has no knowledge of `DictationLanguageStore` or the newer `dictationLanguages` JSON array. `DictationLanguageStore` and `LanguagePickerView` exist only under macos/ (macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Dictation/), not in the shared kit or the iOS target. A grep across ios/ for `DictationLanguageStore`, `LanguagePickerView`, `dictationLanguages`, and `languageStore` returns nothing. The implementation is fully portable: `DictationLanguageStore` imports only `Foundation` + `os` (no platform symbols); `LanguagePickerView` imports only `SwiftUI` + `WaiComputerKit`; both use `Palette`/`Typography`/`Spacing` which are already present in ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift; and `DictationLanguageCatalog` is pure Foundation. The one soft dependency, `OnboardingL10n`, lives only in macOS but wraps the shared-kit `LanguageManager` (available to iOS via WaiComputerKit) and is trivial to port. The `onSelectionChanged` callback calling `dictationManager.prefetchSessionConfigForCurrentLanguage` is a hook specific to the macOS DictationManager; on iOS it would wire to the iOS recording/transcription manager or be a no-op — it is not a blocker. No iOS-unavailable symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Carbon/RegisterEventHotKey, Sparkle, SUUpdater, AX/Accessibility text insertion, menu bar, dock) appear in any of the files involved.

perDomain.title: System-wide dictation (push-to-talk)
perDomain.summary: The domain has 22 discrete capabilities. 5 are already in the shared kit and available to iOS when a host is wired up (ALREADY_PARITY). 10 are hard-blocked by iOS platform constraints (PLATFORM_LIMITED or NOT_APPLICABLE): global modifier-key hotkeys, text insertion into other apps via CGEvent, the always-on-top floating overlay panel, Accessibility TCC permission flow, recovery file path, menu bar extra, and the related onboarding slides. 7 are genuinely PORTABLE gaps requiring new iOS files and store wiring: DictationHistoryStore + DictationHistoryView, DictationDictionaryStore + DictationDictionaryView, DictationLanguageStore / LanguagePickerView (the iOS TranscriptionSettingsView already has a weaker 7-language @AppStorage Picker that should be replaced), the session token prefetch vault, and the dictation language onboarding slide. The AI post-filter toggle is partially present on iOS (the toggle row exists in TranscriptionSettingsView backed by the shared APIClient) but is currently disconnected from any real iOS dictation pipeline. Priority order for portable work: (1) DictationLanguageStore unification with the existing iOS language picker — S effort, already partially wired; (2) DictationHistoryStore + view — M effort, pure Swift with one NSPasteboard → UIPasteboard substitution needed; (3) DictationDictionaryStore + view — M effort, no AppKit dependencies; (4) session token prefetch vault — M effort, needed only once an iOS in-app dictation trigger is designed.

gaps.name: Global push-to-talk hotkey (holds modifier key to dictate into any app while WaiComputer is in background)
gaps.risk: Platform constraint is absolute — no iOS API equivalent for global modifier-key monitoring outside the keyboard extension sandbox.

verdict.capabilityName: Global push-to-talk hotkey (holds modifier key to dictate into any app while WaiComputer is in background)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and not portable. GlobalHotkeyManager.swift (macOS/WaiComputer/Features/Dictation/GlobalHotkeyManager.swift) imports Cocoa, Carbon, IOKit.hid, and ApplicationServices at lines 1-4 — none available on iOS. The global monitor mechanism at lines 611-619 uses NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents(matching: .flagsChanged/.keyDown), which is AppKit-only and has no iOS equivalent: iOS apps cannot intercept input events from other apps while backgrounded. Modifier-key identification uses Carbon kVK_RightOption/kVK_Option/kVK_RightCommand constants (lines 447-451). Permission gating uses AXIsProcessTrusted()/AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions() (lines 68-80) from ApplicationServices — macOS Accessibility TCC, which does not exist on iOS. DictationManager.swift (line 144) instantiates GlobalHotkeyManager and additionally depends on NSRunningApplication (line 158), NSWorkspace.shared.frontmostApplication (line 450), DictationOverlayPanel (an NSPanel subclass, lines 154/1337-1347), and TextInserter (imports Cocoa, uses CGEvent.post). DictationOverlayPanel.swift is a direct NSPanel subclass (Cocoa-only). Neither the shared WaiComputerKit nor any iOS source file contains any hotkey monitoring code — confirmed by exhaustive grep across the repo: NSEvent, addGlobalMonitorForEvents, Carbon, kVK_*, and AXIsProcessTrusted appear exclusively under macos/. There is also no iOS in-app dictation trigger of any kind (no Action Button handler, no UIKeyCommand, no pressesEnded). The defining constraint is architectural: iOS/iPadOS has no public API allowing a background app to observe global input events. A future iOS feature would require a different interaction model (in-app hold button, Action Button shortcut via Shortcuts app, or a custom keyboard extension) and cannot share the GlobalHotkeyManager implementation.
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon framework (import Carbon, kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand, kVK_Function constants)
NSWorkspace (Cocoa/AppKit, macOS-only) — used for frontmostApplication and openApplication
CGEvent.post (Core Graphics, macOS-only) — used in TextInserter for simulated Cmd+V paste

gaps.name: Hands-free dictation mode (double-tap or dedicated hotkey toggles continuous recording until stopped by one press)
gaps.risk: Requires a product decision about an in-app substitute before any iOS implementation can start.

verdict.capabilityName: Hands-free dictation mode (double-tap or dedicated hotkey toggles continuous recording)
verdict.reason: Gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. The hands-free feature (isHandsFree published property at DictationManager.swift:56, setHandsFree() at line 299, onHandsFreeToggle callback at line 383, handsFreeHotkey property at GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:491, double-tap detection via registerTap() at line 809 with doubleTapInterval 0.5 s at line 534) exists exclusively in macOS-specific files. Neither shared/WaiComputerKit nor any iOS file contains these symbols (confirmed by grep returning no matches). The entire trigger mechanism is built on NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents(matching: .flagsChanged) and NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:611,619,631,638) — NSEvent does not exist on iOS. The DictationHotkey enum uses Carbon kVK_* key codes (lines 447–451). The Accessibility TCC gate (AXIsProcessTrusted, lines 68,77,80) and NSRunningApplication.targetApp (DictationManager.swift:158,450) are also macOS-only. An iOS equivalent would need a completely new trigger mechanism (long-press gesture, volume button, or on-screen toggle); the logical state could be abstracted into WaiComputerKit but the detection layer cannot be ported.

gaps.name: Floating overlay panel (always-on-top status bar-level panel shows live transcript + state + duration + cancel while dictating)
gaps.risk: Blocked by iOS window model; an in-app variant (within-app .overlay or sheet) is feasible but only for in-app dictation.

verdict.capabilityName: Floating overlay panel (always-on-top status bar-level panel shows live transcript + state + duration + cancel while dictating)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the classification is correct. DictationOverlayPanel.swift:6 subclasses NSPanel — an AppKit class that does not exist on iOS. The "always-on-top" behavior depends on a chain of macOS-only APIs: NSWindow.Level.statusBar (line 19), NSWindow.CollectionBehavior.canJoinAllSpaces + .fullScreenAuxiliary (line 20), NSPanel.hidesOnDeactivate (line 25), NSHostingView (line 34), orderFrontRegardless/orderOut (lines 41/55), NSAnimationContext.runAnimationGroup (line 42), and NSScreen.main (line 60). On iOS, the OS sandbox prevents any app from placing a window above other apps or above the system status bar — there is no UIKit/SwiftUI equivalent of a process-level floating panel that persists across foreground app changes. Additionally, iOS has no dictation feature at all: there is no DictationManager, no push-to-talk, and no equivalent feature entry point anywhere in the iOS codebase. The SwiftUI view body in DictationOverlayView.swift is itself portable (pure SwiftUI), but the container mechanism that gives it always-on-top, cross-app-space floating behavior is structurally impossible on iOS. Even a weaker in-app-only equivalent (e.g., a SwiftUI .overlay shown while the app is foregrounded during dictation) would first require porting the entire dictation/push-to-talk subsystem to iOS, which is a separate, larger gap.

gaps.name: Text insertion into other apps (clipboard + simulated Cmd+V via CGEvent.post requiring Accessibility permission)
gaps.risk: Absolute platform constraint. No iOS equivalent of CGEvent.post.

verdict.capabilityName: Text insertion into other apps (clipboard + simulated Cmd+V via CGEvent.post requiring Accessibility permission)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed real. TextInserter.swift (macos/.../Dictation/TextInserter.swift) and GlobalHotkeyManager.swift exist only in the macOS target; the iOS client has no equivalent and no shared-kit abstraction covers this capability. Every symbol in the implementation chain is macOS-exclusive: NSPasteboard.general (line 67), NSRunningApplication (lines 62, 106), CGEventSource/CGEvent/CGEventFlags/CGKeyCode/CGEvent.post(tap:.cgSessionEventTap) (lines 120–171), Cocoa import (line 1). The global hotkey machinery in GlobalHotkeyManager.swift adds Carbon kVK_* constants (lines 447–451), IOKit.hid, ApplicationServices, AXIsProcessTrusted/AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions (lines 68–80), NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (lines 611–619), NSWorkspace, NSApp.terminate, and /usr/bin/tccutil shell invocations. None of these APIs exist on iOS. iOS UIPasteboard usages found in TranscriptView.swift:28, RecordingDetailView.swift:454, and McpConnectView.swift:154 are user-initiated copy-to-clipboard actions, not programmatic text injection into third-party apps. The capability is fundamentally blocked by iOS's sandboxing model: iOS apps cannot post synthetic keyboard events to other processes, and there is no UIPasteboard + UIEvent equivalent of CGEvent.post(tap:.cgSessionEventTap). The correct iOS analogy (Share Sheet, copy-to-clipboard with in-app notification) is a different user interaction model, not a port of the same mechanism.

gaps.name: Accessibility + microphone permission flow (TCC prompts, status banners, one-time legacy TCC migration, stale-needs-restart state)
gaps.risk: Not applicable to iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Accessibility + microphone permission flow (TCC prompts, status banners, one-time legacy TCC migration, stale-needs-restart state)
verdict.reason: The first-pass classification is confirmed correct. The macOS capability is a multi-part TCC permission system that has no iOS equivalent. (1) Accessibility TCC (AXIsProcessTrusted / AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions at GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:68-81): iOS has no Accessibility TCC concept at all — there is no global-keyboard-monitor permission on iOS. (2) Global keyboard monitor (NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents at line 611-625): iOS provides no API to monitor keystrokes in other apps; this is the entire mechanism for system-wide push-to-talk detection. (3) Legacy TCC migration via tccutil shell commands (lines 156-178): tccutil is a macOS-only CLI binary; Process() execution is meaningless on iOS. (4) Relaunch-after-migration dance using NSWorkspace.shared.openApplication + NSApp.terminate (lines 193-204): NSWorkspace and NSApp do not exist on iOS. (5) staleNeedsRestart enum case (line 62) and its corresponding Settings banner (MacSettingsView.swift:374-381): iOS permission changes take effect immediately without requiring a relaunch, so this state has no iOS analogue. The iOS app does have a microphone permission flow (AVAudioApplication.requestRecordPermission() at RecordingViewModel.swift:127 and AudioManager.swift:24, plus an onboarding gate at OnboardingView.swift:158-167), but that covers only the microphone subset and is already present — it is not a gap. The Accessibility + hotkey + TCC-migration + stale-restart portions are entirely absent on iOS and cannot be ported because every load-bearing symbol is macOS-only.
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon framework (kVK_RightOption, kVK_RightCommand, kVK_Option, kVK_Control, kVK_Function — macOS-only)
CGEvent.post (CoreGraphics, macOS-only, referenced in comments at lines 83-88)

gaps.name: Dictation feature enable/disable toggle (persisted to UserDefaults, gates hotkey and AudioEngineHost prewarm)
gaps.risk: Depends on first implementing in-app dictation trigger (platform-limited core capability).

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation feature enable/disable toggle (persisted to UserDefaults, gates hotkey and AudioEngineHost prewarm)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS has no DictationManager, no isFeatureEnabled toggle, and no dictation push-to-talk feature at all. The only iOS dictation reference is a `dictationPostFilterEnabled` toggle in `ios/.../SettingsView.swift:243`, which is an unrelated server-side AI post-processing switch — it does not correspond to the feature enable/disable being evaluated. The macOS `isFeatureEnabled` property (DictationManager.swift:104–119) is not portable in isolation because its `didSet` unconditionally calls `applyHotkeyAvailability()` (line 108), which starts/stops `GlobalHotkeyManager` and `AudioEngineHost`. `GlobalHotkeyManager` is defined in `macos/.../GlobalHotkeyManager.swift` and directly imports `Carbon`, `IOKit.hid`, and `ApplicationServices` — all macOS-only frameworks — and uses `CGEventTap`, Carbon `RegisterEventHotKey`, and `NSApp`/`NSWorkspace`. Additionally, `DictationManager` holds `DictationOverlayPanel` (a subclass of `NSPanel`, AppKit-only), `NSRunningApplication` (macOS-only), `NSWorkspace.shared.frontmostApplication` (macOS-only), `NSSound` for auditory feedback (macOS-only), and `TextInserter` which uses `CGEvent`/`CGEventSource`/`CGEventTap` to simulate Cmd+V paste into a target application — a concept that has no iOS equivalent since iOS does not support cross-app keyboard event injection. The feature as a whole is a desktop dictation UX (global hotkey → mic capture → streaming STT → paste into frontmost app) that is architecturally macOS-specific. A future iOS in-app dictation equivalent would require a fully separate design using an in-app trigger, UIKit/SwiftUI text field insertion, and no cross-app paste — not a port of the existing toggle.
hiddenPlatformDeps: TextInserter — clipboard + simulated Cmd+V via CGEvent.post into another app (TextInserter.swift:31-50)

gaps.name: Push-to-talk hotkey picker (choose from Right Option, Left Option, Right Command, Fn, Ctrl+Option)
gaps.risk: Not applicable on iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Push-to-talk hotkey picker (choose from Right Option, Left Option, Right Command, Fn, Ctrl+Option)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and the classification stands. DictationHotkey (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413-462) is defined inside the macOS-only target with direct imports of Carbon (kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand at lines 447-451), NSEvent.ModifierFlags (line 456), and the file opens with `import Cocoa; import Carbon; import IOKit.hid; import ApplicationServices` — none of which exist on iOS. GlobalHotkeyManager itself (line 472+) drives detection via NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents and NSEvent.addLocalMonitorForEvents (lines 611, 619, 631, 638) and pastes via CGEvent.post — all macOS-only APIs. The shared WaiComputerKit has no DictationHotkey type and no hotkey-selection logic; "push-to-talk" appears only in comments on session/audio orchestration classes. The iOS SettingsView (ios/.../SettingsView.swift) has zero hotkey or picker UI; the only dictation-adjacent control is a server-side post-filter toggle in TranscriptionSettingsView. Beyond missing code, the feature concept itself does not apply to iOS: Right Option, Right Command, and Fn are physical modifier keys on macOS keyboards with no equivalent iOS interaction primitive, and iOS provides no API for system-wide modifier-key interception while the app is backgrounded.

gaps.name: Hands-free hotkey picker (choose separate key for hands-free toggle, or default to double-tap)
gaps.risk: Not applicable on iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Hands-free hotkey picker (choose separate key for hands-free toggle, or default to double-tap)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the capability is not portable to iOS. DictationHotkey (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413) is macOS-only: its keyCode property uses Carbon kVK_RightOption / kVK_Option / kVK_RightCommand / kVK_Function constants (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:446-452), and requiredFlags returns NSEvent.ModifierFlags (line 456) — both AppKit/Carbon-exclusive. GlobalHotkeyManager itself imports Carbon, IOKit.hid, and ApplicationServices, and drives everything through NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents (lines 611, 619) which requires the macOS Accessibility TCC permission (AXIsProcessTrusted). None of these APIs exist on iOS. Furthermore, the concept is architecturally void on iOS: the picker lets users choose which physical modifier key (Right Option, Right Command, Fn, Control+Option) triggers hands-free dictation system-wide — iOS has no global keyboard hook surface and those physical keys do not exist on the iPhone. The iOS client has no DictationManager, no GlobalHotkeyManager, no DictationHotkey, and no hotkey UI of any kind; its only dictation-related setting is a post-filter toggle (ios SettingsView.swift:242-243).

gaps.name: AI post-filter (cleanup) toggle — enable/disable LLM post-processing of transcribed text, server-synced setting
gaps.risk: Low — server wiring already exists; risk is that the setting has no effect until an iOS dictation pipeline is added.

verdict.capabilityName: AI post-filter (cleanup) toggle — enable/disable LLM post-processing of transcribed text, server-synced setting
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed but the classification should be PLATFORM_LIMITED, not PORTABLE. On macOS, DictationManager.swift:262 ingests `settings.dictationPostFilterEnabled` into `aiCleanupEnabled`, and lines 744–770 gate a live call to `apiClient.cleanupDictation(text:vocabulary:)` inside `stopAndInsert()`. The setting has real effect because a full in-app dictation pipeline exists (GlobalHotkeyManager, AudioEngineHost, DictationOverlayPanel, TextInserter). On iOS, SettingsView.swift:242–280 shows a toggle that reads/writes `UserSettings.dictationPostFilterEnabled` via `APIClient.updateSettings` — server sync works — but no iOS file anywhere references `DictationManager`, `GlobalHotkeyManager`, `cleanupDictation`, `TextInserter`, or `DictationOverlayPanel` (verified by exhaustive search). The toggle is a dangling preference: it persists to the server but nothing on iOS reads it or acts on it. This is not simply a missing UI wire-up. The upstream blocker is that iOS has no in-app push-to-talk dictation pipeline at all, and implementing one requires iOS-specific alternatives for every macOS-only symbol in the pipeline: global hotkeys use Carbon/NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents/CGEventTap (all macOS-only); text insertion uses NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post into NSRunningApplication (macOS-only); the overlay uses NSPanel (macOS-only); audio pre-roll uses AudioEngineHost whose prewarm/lease pattern would need iOS-compatible backing. The setting toggle itself is portable SwiftUI + shared APIClient, but making it functional on iOS is blocked by the absence of an iOS dictation pipeline — which requires a platform-specific design (e.g., a dedicated in-app recording button or share-extension trigger rather than a system-wide hotkey).

gaps.name: Dictation history screen (list of past dictations grouped by day, with stats: total words, average WPM, streak days, search, copy, delete)
gaps.risk: Medium — DictationHistoryStore is straightforward to port but needs wiring at app startup (attach/hydrate on login, clearLocalCache on logout); missing that wiring would silently leave history unsynced.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation history screen (list of past dictations grouped by day, with stats: total words, average WPM, streak days, search, copy, delete)
verdict.reason: Gap is real. iOS has no DictationHistoryView, no DictationHistoryStore, and no navigation entry for either (confirmed by exhaustive grep across ios/). The macOS implementation lives at macos/.../Features/Dictation/DictationHistoryView.swift and DictationHistoryStore.swift. The store is fully portable: it imports only Foundation + WaiComputerKit, persists via FileManager/applicationSupportDirectory (cross-platform), and uses APIClient.listDictationEntries/createDictationEntry/deleteDictationEntry which are defined in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Network/APIClient.swift:1037-1047. ContentUnavailableViewCompat is in the shared kit and declares iOS 17 support. The iOS DesignSystem.swift already has matching Spacing, Typography, and Palette enums. Three shallow symbols block a direct port but are not architectural barriers: (1) NSPasteboard (lines 192-193 of DictationHistoryView.swift) — must be replaced with UIPasteboard.general on iOS; (2) MacDateFormatting (lines 121 and 174) — defined only in macos/DesignSystem.swift:161, needs an equivalent date-formatting helper in the iOS DesignSystem; (3) OnboardingL10n (lines 153, 159) — macOS-only localization helper, needs an iOS equivalent or a shared abstraction. None of these require OS-level entitlements or unavailable frameworks — all are straightforward SwiftUI/Foundation substitutions.

MacDateFormatting — used at DictationHistoryView.swift:121,174; defined only in macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Core/DesignSystem.swift:161; not present in iOS DesignSystem.swift

OnboardingL10n — used at DictationHistoryView.swift:153,159 for the t() localization helper and dictationsCountText(); defined only in macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingL10n.swift; not present anywhere in the iOS target

gaps.name: Custom dictionary screen (add/delete vocabulary bias words and auto-correction pairs, search, overuse warning, server-synced)
gaps.risk: Medium — same lifecycle wiring risk as history store (attach/hydrate/clearLocalCache); dictionary entries must flow to in-app dictation sessions to have any effect.

verdict.capabilityName: Custom dictionary screen (add/delete vocabulary bias words and auto-correction pairs, search, overuse warning, server-synced)
verdict.reason: Gap is confirmed and the implementation is fully portable. iOS has zero dictionary-related code: grep across all iOS Swift files finds no DictationDictionaryStore, DictationDictionaryView, or DictionaryWord references, and SettingsView.swift (lines 36-183) has no dictionary navigation entry. The macOS DictationDictionaryView.swift imports only SwiftUI and WaiComputerKit; DictationDictionaryStore.swift imports only Foundation, os, and WaiComputerKit. No AppKit/Cocoa/NSPanel/NSWindow/Carbon/CGEvent/Sparkle/CATap symbol appears anywhere in either file. All three server API methods (listDictationDictionary, createDictionaryWord, deleteDictionaryWord at APIClient.swift:1054-1068) and all DTOs (DictionaryWordDTO, CreateDictionaryWordRequest in Dictation.swift:82-134) are already in the shared WaiComputerKit and available to iOS. ContentUnavailableViewCompat is in the shared kit (Compat/ContentUnavailableViewCompat.swift). LanguageManager is in the shared kit. Spacing/Typography/Palette are in iOS Core/DesignSystem.swift. The only friction is OnboardingL10n (macos/.../App/Onboarding/OnboardingL10n.swift), a 30-line enum with no macOS dependencies, used by DictationDictionaryView for its t() helper — this needs to be either added to the iOS target or trivially inlined. FileManager.applicationSupportDirectory (Store line 44) is fully available on iOS. The proposed target files (DictationDictionaryStore.swift, DictationDictionaryView.swift, SettingsView.swift entry) are correct.

gaps.name: Dictation language picker (auto-detect vs single BCP-47 language for STT hint, 15-language catalog, persisted per device)
gaps.risk: Low — store and view are already iOS-compatible; main risk is ensuring DictationLanguageStore's legacy migration correctly handles the @AppStorage('transcriptionLanguage') value the iOS app may have written.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation language picker (auto-detect vs single BCP-47 language, 15-language catalog, persisted per device)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS TranscriptionSettingsView (SettingsView.swift:210–235) uses @AppStorage("transcriptionLanguage") with a plain Picker holding 7 hardcoded language options. It writes the legacy string "multi" for auto-detect — not the new empty-set contract — and does not use DictationLanguageStore at all. RecordingViewModel.swift:134 still reads the legacy UserDefaults key directly. DictationLanguageStore, DictationLanguageCatalog (15 entries), and LanguagePickerView exist only under the macOS target. However, both DictationLanguageStore and LanguagePickerView are pure Swift/SwiftUI with zero macOS-exclusive symbols: no NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, Carbon, Sparkle, SUUpdater, AX/Accessibility APIs, menu bar, or dock references were found. LanguagePickerView has two soft dependencies: LanguageManager (already in shared/WaiComputerKit) and OnboardingL10n (currently macOS-only but itself pure Foundation+WaiComputerKit with no macOS symbols). iOS does not currently inject LanguageManager into the SwiftUI environment, but it is available in WaiComputerKit. The migration path is: move DictationLanguageStore+DictationLanguageCatalog to the shared kit or duplicate into the iOS target, move or duplicate OnboardingL10n, wire LanguageManager.shared as an environment object in the iOS app root, and replace TranscriptionSettingsView's Picker with LanguagePickerView backed by DictationLanguageStore.

gaps.name: Session token prefetching and vault (pre-warms a Deepgram realtime STT token 20s before the hotkey is pressed to minimize first-press latency)
gaps.risk: Medium — requires an iOS DictationManager host; the prefetch logic itself is clean but the cost-guard (idle refresh cap) must be carefully replicated to avoid Deepgram token runaway on iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Session token prefetching and vault for push-to-talk dictation
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. On macOS, DictationManager.swift:135-136 holds a sessionConfigVault: RealtimeTranscriptionSessionConfigVault? instantiated in configure() (line 223), with prefetchDictationSessionConfig() called on configure/settings_ingest/idle_after_session (lines 234, 272, 1078) and scheduleDictationSessionConfigRefresh() looping every 20s with 15 max idle refreshes (lines 177, 181, 902-953). On iOS there is no IOSDictationManager.swift, no equivalent manager class, and no wiring of the vault anywhere — the only iOS uses of createRealtimeTranscriptionSession are on-demand in RecordingViewModel.swift:165 for .recording purpose with no prefetch. The RealtimeTranscriptionSessionConfigVault itself (imports only Foundation, pure Swift actor) and createRealtimeTranscriptionSession in shared APIClient.swift:1305 are both portable. However, the integration point — an iOS dictation manager that drives prefetch — does not exist, and the triggering mechanism (GlobalHotkeyManager) depends on Carbon/NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents/NSRunningApplication/NSWorkspace/NSPanel/AXIsProcessTrusted(), all of which are unavailable on iOS. iOS has no concept of a system-wide push-to-talk hotkey: there is no way to monitor global modifier keys in background, no NSPanel overlay, no Accessibility TCC permission for event posting. A portable iOS dictation panel could wire the vault to a SwiftUI button trigger instead of a hotkey, but that is a new design, not a straight port. The capability as specified (pre-warm token 20s before the hotkey fires) is platform-limited: the vault subcomponent is portable but the entire push-to-talk + prefetch orchestration layer is macOS-only in its current architecture.

verdict.capabilityName: Recovery text file (saves dictated text to Application Support when paste fails, with copy-kept error message)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and the original PLATFORM_LIMITED classification holds. iOS has no push-to-talk dictation feature at all — there is no DictationManager, no TextInserter, and no mechanism to insert text into third-party apps. The recovery file (DictationManager.swift:1311-1332) exists solely as a fallback when TextInserter.insert() throws (line 807-810), which uses NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post(tap:.cgSessionEventTap) to simulate Cmd+V into the frontmost app. Every symbol in that insertion path — NSPasteboard, NSRunningApplication, CGEvent, CGKeyCode, CGEventSource, CGEventFlags, .cgSessionEventTap, MacInputPermission, MacPrivacySettings — is macOS-only and unavailable on iOS. The saveRecoveryText function body itself (FileManager, ISO8601DateFormatter, String.write) uses only Foundation APIs portable to iOS, but the function can never be called on iOS because the primary feature (inter-app text insertion) is architecturally prohibited by iOS sandboxing. Porting the recovery file in isolation would be meaningless without porting TextInserter, which cannot be ported.

gaps.name: Menu-bar dictation shortcut (last dictation preview shown in menu bar popover)
gaps.risk: Not applicable.

verdict.capabilityName: Menu-bar dictation shortcut (last dictation preview shown in menu bar popover)
verdict.reason: The first-pass classification is correct. The entire capability is structurally bound to the macOS menu bar: WaiComputerMacApp.swift:248 wraps MenuBarView inside a SwiftUI MenuBarExtra scene — a macOS-only construct. MenuBarView.swift:223–226 reads historyStore.entries.first and copies to NSPasteboard — AppKit-only. MenuBarView.swift:283,307 calls NSApplication.shared.terminate(nil), and line 317 uses MacPresentationCoordinator. DictationHistoryStore and DictationManager live exclusively under macos/WaiComputer/ with zero references in the iOS target or shared WaiComputerKit. iOS has no push-to-talk, no DictationManager, and no DictationHistoryStore wiring at all. There is no equivalent persistent ambient surface on iOS that could host a "last dictation preview" — the concept of a status-bar popover does not exist on that platform. This is correctly NOT_APPLICABLE, not a gap.
hiddenPlatformDeps: DictationHistoryStore (macOS-only class, not in shared WaiComputerKit)
DictationManager (macOS-only class, not in shared WaiComputerKit)

gaps.name: Dictation onboarding — hotkey picker slide (choose PTT key during first launch)
gaps.risk: Not applicable on iOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation onboarding — hotkey picker slide (choose PTT key during first launch)
verdict.reason:
The gap is real and the feature is not portable to iOS. Evidence:

1. `DictationHotkey` (enum with `allCases` iterated by the slide) is defined entirely inside macOS/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Dictation/GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413 — it lives in the macOS app target, not in shared/WaiComputerKit. It has no counterpart in iOS or in the shared kit.

2. `DictationHotkey.keyCode` (GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:447-451) imports Carbon and returns `UInt16(kVK_RightOption)`, `kVK_Option`, `kVK_RightCommand` — Carbon virtual key codes that are iOS-unavailable.

3. `DictationHotkey.requiredFlags` (line 456) uses `NSEvent.ModifierFlags` — iOS-unavailable.

4. The underlying engine `GlobalHotkeyManager` (imports: Cocoa, Carbon, IOKit.hid, ApplicationServices) uses `NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents`, `CGEvent.post`, `NSWorkspace`, and `CGEventTap` — none of which exist on iOS.

5. `DictationManager.updateHotkey(_:)` (called by the slide button) ultimately programs the `GlobalHotkeyManager`; this class has no iOS analog.

6. `OnboardingHotkeyPickerSlide.swift:75` references `Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor)` — `NSColor` is AppKit-only.

7. The concept itself — a global physical keyboard modifier key (right Option/Command/Fn/Control+Option) held for push-to-talk — is architecturally impossible on iOS. iOS has no global keyboard hook, no Carbon key codes, and no CGEvent injection. iOS dictation is triggered by the system microphone button or an in-app tap, not a hardware hotkey.

The iOS OnboardingView.swift confirms the absence: it iterates `OnboardingPage.allCases` with no `.hotkey` case, renders only `.permission` and `.voiceSetup` slides, and imports UIKit (not AppKit).

First-pass classification PLATFORM_LIMITED is correct.
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon (kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand, kVK_Function — Carbon virtual key codes)

gaps.name: Dictation onboarding — language picker slide (pick STT language during first launch)
gaps.risk: Low — depends only on DictationLanguageStore port (separate gap above).

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation onboarding — language picker slide (pick STT language during first launch)
verdict.reason:
The gap is real. iOS OnboardingPage (ios/.../OnboardingPage.swift) has no `.languages` case and no language-picker slide anywhere in the iOS onboarding flow — confirmed by grepping the entire ios/ tree for LanguagePickerView, DictationLanguageStore, and dictationLanguage, all of which return zero hits. The macOS OnboardingView (macos/.../OnboardingView.swift:103) switches on `.languages` to show OnboardingLanguagesSlide, which wraps LanguagePickerView(store:).

One blocking platform dependency exists: OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift line 46 uses Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor), an AppKit symbol unavailable on iOS. This is a trivial fix — replace with Color(.systemBackground) or a Palette token. All other code in OnboardingLanguagesSlide, LanguagePickerView, and DictationLanguageStore is SwiftUI + Foundation + UserDefaults with no further AppKit/Carbon/CATap/Accessibility/Sparkle symbols. DictationManager is referenced only for the optional prefetchSessionConfigForCurrentLanguage callback; iOS can pass a no-op closure or skip that call since dictation push-to-talk doesn't exist on iOS.

The three files needing work are macOS-local (not in shared/WaiComputerKit), so they must be ported: DictationLanguageStore and DictationLanguageCatalog (pure Foundation — trivially portable), LanguagePickerView (pure SwiftUI — trivially portable), and a new iOS OnboardingLanguagesSlide that replaces NSColor.windowBackgroundColor with a platform-safe color. The iOS OnboardingPage enum needs a new .languages case and OnboardingView needs to render it.

gaps.name: Dictation onboarding — sandbox try-it-now slide (live dictation demo inside onboarding, shows lastFinalTranscript)
gaps.risk: Blocked by platform-limited push-to-talk core.

verdict.capabilityName: Dictation onboarding — sandbox try-it-now slide (live dictation demo inside onboarding, shows lastFinalTranscript)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and the feature is not portable as-is. The macOS slide (OnboardingDictationSandboxSlide.swift) takes a DictationManager as a direct parameter (line 8), and DictationManager lives exclusively at macos/…/Features/Dictation/DictationManager.swift — it is absent from shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/ entirely. DictationManager's core engine GlobalHotkeyManager imports Carbon, Cocoa, IOKit.hid, and ApplicationServices; it uses Carbon kVK_* key codes, NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents for global modifier-key capture, CGEvent.post for automated Cmd+V paste, and AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions for the Accessibility TCC gate — none of which exist on iOS. DictationManager also holds a DictationOverlayPanel (NSPanel) and calls TextInserter, both macOS-only. The sandbox slide itself has one additional macOS-only symbol: Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor) at line 133. iOS has no push-to-talk dictation concept at all: its onboarding (OnboardingPage.swift) defines five pages (welcome, record, transcribe, permission, voiceSetup) with no sandbox case, and OnboardingView.swift never instantiates a DictationManager or a sandbox slide. A portable equivalent would require a fully new iOS-native dictation flow (long-press button or similar) backed by a new iOS DictationManager that does not depend on Carbon/CGEvent/NSEvent global monitors, which is non-trivial re-architecture rather than a simple backport.
hiddenPlatformDeps: CGEvent.post — macOS Accessibility event posting for automated Cmd+V paste
NSWorkspace — used in DictationManager:450 and GlobalHotkeyManager
GlobalHotkeyManager — entire class is macOS-only (CGEventTap / NSEvent global monitor path)

perDomain.title: Billing & subscription
perDomain.summary: macOS has a complete billing and subscription management UI in BillingSection.swift (read-only status + usage + upgrade + promo + cancel + polling), wired into MacSettingsView. iOS has zero billing capabilities — the Settings screen contains no subscription status, no usage display, and no payment controls. The shared BillingClient and BillingModels are available to both platforms. The core split is: read-only status/usage display and cancel-subscription are cleanly PORTABLE via the shared kit; the upgrade/purchase flow is DIFFERENT_BY_DESIGN because App Store rules require StoreKit IAP for iOS digital-goods purchases, blocking a direct port of the Stripe/Tinkoff web-checkout path. The promo code flow is partially blocked for the same reason (its checkout-fallback branch cannot go through a web URL on iOS). All polling and app-return refresh logic is technically portable but is gated on the upgrade architecture decision.

Neither blocker affects the status-display portion (planLine + statusBadge); those two functions are pure SwiftUI with no macOS-specific symbols and can be ported directly to ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Billing/BillingStatusSection.swift.

The proControls() UI block (BillingSection.swift:319-345) is pure SwiftUI (Button, ProgressView, Text, HStack, Spacer) with no AppKit or macOS-only APIs. The platform dependencies in the wider BillingSection struct (import AppKit line 1, MacAppState line 45, NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) line 511, MacDateFormatting line 623) belong to the checkout flow and date formatting helpers, not to the cancel path. A new iOS view can wire the same cancelBillingSubscription() call through the iOS AppState (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/WaiComputerApp.swift:224) using plain SwiftUI. No blocking iOS-unavailable symbol is required for the cancel capability itself.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace (BillingSection.swift:511, checkout flow only — not the cancel path)
MacAppState (BillingSection.swift:45, macOS-specific ObservableObject — iOS has its own AppState)
AppKit (BillingSection.swift:1, imported by the macOS file — not needed for a new iOS view)
MacDateFormatting (BillingSection.swift:623, macOS-only date formatter — not used in the cancel path)

gaps.name: Renewal date and cancel-at-period-end 'Pro through date' display
gaps.risk: Low — read-only display of existing model fields.

verdict.capabilityName: Renewal date and cancel-at-period-end 'Pro through date' display
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. The iOS Settings screen (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift) has no Billing section at all — no subscription status, no currentPeriodEnd, no cancelAtPeriodEnd display. The proposed target file ios/.../Billing/BillingStatusSection.swift does not exist. The shared kit (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Billing/BillingModels.swift:137-181) exposes BillingSubscription with both currentPeriodEnd: Date? and cancelAtPeriodEnd: Bool as public Sendable types, fully usable on iOS. The specific proControls function in BillingSection.swift:319-345 that renders "Pro through <date>" / "Renews on <date>" uses only plain SwiftUI (Text, HStack, Button, ProgressView, .font, .foregroundStyle) and the shared BillingSubscription model — no macOS-only symbols. The broader BillingSection.swift file does contain macOS-only code (import AppKit at line 1, NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) at line 511 for the checkout URL, and MacAppState as the environment object type at line 45), but those are confined to the checkout-initiation flow, not to the date-display sub-function. An iOS BillingStatusSection.swift implementing only the renewal/cancel-at-period-end display can use pure SwiftUI + WaiComputerKit with no blocking platform dependency. The checkout link would need UIApplication/openURL on iOS, but that is a separate capability from the date display being evaluated here.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace (line 511: NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) — macOS-only checkout URL opener, not part of the date-display sub-function but present in the same file)
AppKit (import AppKit line 1 — macOS-only framework import in BillingSection.swift)

gaps.name: Post-checkout subscription refresh polling (periodic re-fetch after opening checkout URL)
gaps.risk: Low in isolation; blocked by upgrade flow architecture decision.

verdict.capabilityName: Post-checkout subscription refresh polling
verdict.reason: Gap is real and confirmed. iOS has no billing UI at all: no BillingStatusSection.swift exists at the proposed target path, no iOS Swift file imports any billing type from the shared kit (BillingSubscription, BillingPlan, BillingUsage, getBillingSubscription), and the iOS SettingsView (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift) has no billing section. The polling mechanism itself — startCheckoutRefreshPolling/stopCheckoutRefreshPolling at BillingSection.swift:573-601, the checkoutRefreshTask: Task<Void,Never> state, BillingCheckoutRefreshStore.pendingKey AppStorage key (defined in macos/.../PaymentModeToggle.swift:36-38), and the sequential delay array at line 72-79 — uses only Task, Task.sleep(nanoseconds:), @MainActor, and @AppStorage, all of which are available on iOS via SwiftUI/Swift Concurrency. The single blocking dependency is NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) at BillingSection.swift:511, which is AppKit-only and is what triggers the polling (checkoutRefreshPending=true + startCheckoutRefreshPolling() at lines 517-518). On iOS this must be replaced with SwiftUI's @Environment(\.openURL) or UIApplication.shared.open. Once that substitution is made, the entire polling pattern can land on iOS with no other changes needed.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) at BillingSection.swift:511 (AppKit-only; iOS equivalent is @Environment(\.openURL) or UIApplication.shared.open)

gaps.name: App-return billing refresh (re-fetches subscription on scene becoming active if checkout pending)
gaps.risk: Low — same SwiftUI scenePhase API; only relevant after upgrade flow architecture is resolved.
