verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed real. iOS has zero billing infrastructure — no BillingSection, no BillingCheckoutRefreshStore usage, no billingCheckoutRefreshPending AppStorage key anywhere under ios/. The iOS scenePhase .active handler (WaiComputerApp.swift:212-217) calls only resumePendingRecordingSyncIfNeeded(), not any billing refresh. The refreshBillingOnReturnIfNeeded() logic itself (MacSettingsView.swift:1188-1207) is pure SwiftUI/Swift Task/Task.sleep/AppStorage and would be portable in isolation, but it is gated by the existence of a checkout flow that sets billingCheckoutRefreshPending=true. That checkout flow lives entirely in BillingSection.swift, which imports AppKit (line 1) and uses NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) (line 511) to open the checkout URL — a macOS-only API with no iOS equivalent. Until billing UI is built for iOS and the URL-open call is replaced with UIApplication.shared.open(url), the entire capability — including the refresh hook — cannot land on iOS. The classification is PLATFORM_LIMITED rather than PORTABLE because the dependency that triggers the refresh (NSWorkspace-based checkout open) is iOS-unavailable and is not a trivial swap.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSWorkspace (NSWorkspace.shared.open(url) in BillingSection.swift:511 — no iOS equivalent; must be replaced with UIApplication.shared.open)
AppKit (imported at BillingSection.swift:1 — iOS does not have AppKit)

gaps.name: Localized billing error messages (promo code errors resolved against app language, not system locale)
gaps.risk: Low — copy the t() helper pattern already used on macOS.

verdict.capabilityName: Localized billing error messages (promo code errors resolved against app language, not system locale)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. iOS has no billing UI whatsoever — there is no `ios/.../Features/Billing/` directory, no promo code handling, and no subscription screens in any of the 22 iOS Swift files. The macOS implementation (`BillingSection.swift:630-668`) contains `localizedBillingActionError()` which calls the private `t()` helper at line 666-668, which delegates to `OnboardingL10n.text(english, russian, language: languageManager.current)`. The `LanguageManager` type is in the shared kit (`shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift`) and is fully portable — pure `Foundation`+`SwiftUI`, no macOS-only APIs. `OnboardingL10n` itself is defined in `macos/.../App/Onboarding/OnboardingL10n.swift` (macOS-only target), but it contains only pure Swift logic (`Foundation` import, `Locale.preferredLanguages`, string switching) — there are no macOS-only symbols. The localization pattern is therefore portable: `LanguageManager` is already in the shared kit; `OnboardingL10n` or equivalent logic can be placed there or recreated identically in iOS. The two platform-specific dependencies present in the broader `BillingSection` struct — `import AppKit` (line 1) and `MacAppState`/`MacDateFormatting` — are in the surrounding billing view, not in the `localizedBillingActionError()` function itself. When iOS eventually gains a billing screen, porting this specific error-localization pattern requires only: sharing or duplicating `OnboardingL10n.text()` logic, injecting `LanguageManager` as an `@EnvironmentObject`, and copying the switch statement. No iOS-unavailable symbols block it.

perDomain.title: Wai companion / assistant
perDomain.summary:
The Wai companion / assistant domain achieves near-complete structural parity through the shared CompanionView in WaiComputerKit, which both platforms instantiate. The full companion experience — SSE streaming, chat list, rename/delete, citation chips, starter prompts, tool-call notes, auto-scroll, cancel — is implemented once in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Views/CompanionView.swift and runs identically on both platforms (with explicit #if os(macOS) layout branches for the sidebar vs stacked chat panel).

iOS is NOT behind on core functionality. iOS actually has a dedicated first-class tab for the companion (MainTabView tag 2), while macOS surfaces it as a sidebar section. Three small wiring omissions exist in WaiHomeView.swift (14 lines total): (1) recordings: [] is passed instead of a populated list, so citation chips always show the fallback label "Recording"; (2) the companionAccentColor modifier is not applied, so the companion uses the system accent color instead of Palette.accent; (3) the locale environment override from LanguageManager is absent. A fourth gap is that iOS has no handler for the 'navigateTo wai' NotificationCenter deep-link that macOS wires. All four are S-effort portable fixes with no platform dependency. No PLATFORM_LIMITED or NOT_APPLICABLE gaps exist for companion functionality itself.

gaps.name: Citation title resolution (recording name shown in chip)
gaps.risk: Requires AppState to expose recordings or WaiHomeView to own a fetch task; light risk of redundant API call if library is already loaded elsewhere.

verdict.capabilityName: Citation title resolution (recording name shown in chip)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and the first-pass classification is correct. formattedCitation() lives entirely in the shared kit at CompanionView.swift:863-869 and contains no platform-specific symbols — it does a pure Swift array lookup on the recordings parameter. On macOS (MacContentView.swift:851) that parameter receives libraryViewModel.recordings, a live-populated [Recording] from MacLibraryViewModel. On iOS (WaiHomeView.swift:10) recordings: [] is passed explicitly; the comment confirms intent. The iOS LibraryViewModel (LibraryView.swift:643) does hold a populated recordings array but is a @StateObject scoped inside LibraryView and is never accessible from WaiHomeView. MainTabView (ContentView.swift:39-114) and AppState (WaiComputerApp.swift:224-485) expose no recordings list. The fix requires only lifting or sharing the recordings array into WaiHomeView — either by hoisting LibraryViewModel to MainTabView/AppState or by giving WaiHomeView its own instance — all pure SwiftUI + shared-kit work, no macOS-gated API involved.

gaps.name: Deep-link navigation to Wai section via NotificationCenter
gaps.risk: Low — TabView integer tag is stable (defined in ContentView.swift:82-83); just add an onReceive for the same notification name.

verdict.capabilityName: Deep-link navigation to Wai section via NotificationCenter
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. MacContentView.swift:496-507 registers an onReceive handler for the "navigateTo" NotificationCenter notification and maps target "wai" to selectedSection = .wai. iOS ContentView.swift (and MainTabView within it) has no equivalent handler — the only NotificationCenter listener in the iOS file is for .pendingRecordingRecoveryNotice (line 88). The Wai tab exists on iOS as tab index 2 (WaiHomeView, ContentView.swift:59-63) with @AppStorage("selectedTab"), so the routing target is present. The macOS handler implementation uses only NotificationCenter.default, onReceive (SwiftUI), and a @State assignment — zero macOS-only symbols. No NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, CommandMenu, MenuBarExtra, Sparkle, CATap, CGEvent, or other platform-exclusive types are in the handler path (lines 496-507). The producers of this notification in WaiComputerMacApp.swift use CommandMenu/CommandGroup (macOS-only), but those are irrelevant to portability of the receiver side. The fix is to add .onReceive(NotificationCenter.default.publisher(for: .init("navigateTo"))) to MainTabView mapping "wai" to selectedTab = 2. One additional note: no code currently posts navigateTo with "wai" as the target on either platform — the macOS switch case at line 504 is a dead handler. The iOS gap is real, but so is the macOS handler being unused.

gaps.name: companionAccentColor environment injection
gaps.risk: Cosmetic only; requires knowing the iOS design token for accent color (Palette.accent is defined in shared kit).

verdict.capabilityName: companionAccentColor environment injection
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed but the original classification is correct. macOS (MacContentView.swift:854) injects .companionAccentColor(Palette.accent) where Palette.accent is a user-configurable accent resolved from MacThemePreferences (DesignSystem.swift:192). iOS (WaiHomeView.swift:10) calls CompanionView with no .companionAccentColor modifier, so the environment key falls back to its defaultValue (.accentColor, CompanionView.swift:5). The modifier and environment key are both defined in the shared kit (CompanionView.swift:4-19) with no platform guards and no AppKit/macOS-only symbols anywhere in the injection path. The only blocker to parity is that iOS has no equivalent of Palette.accent to pass — iOS would need to define its own accent constant (e.g. Color.accentColor or a brand constant) and inject it in WaiHomeView. That is pure SwiftUI and shared-kit work with no hidden platform dependencies.

gaps.name: Locale / date formatting injection into CompanionView
gaps.risk: Low — users with system locale matching app language are unaffected; only mis-match between system and in-app language setting causes wrong date formatting.

verdict.capabilityName: Locale / date formatting injection into CompanionView
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed and genuine. On macOS, MacContentView.swift:853 applies .environment(\.locale, MacDateFormatting.locale(for: languageManager.current)) directly on the CompanionView instantiation, so CompanionView.swift:68 (@Environment(\.locale)) receives the user's in-app language preference and threads it through RelativeDateTimeFormatter (line 858) and DateFormatter via chatLabel (line 842). On iOS, WaiComputerApp has no locale injection, ContentView has no locale injection, MainTabView has no locale injection, and WaiHomeView.swift:10 calls CompanionView with no .environment(\.locale, ...) modifier. LanguageManagedRoot (defined in shared/WaiComputerKit/.../LanguageManager.swift:128-140, which does inject manager.preferredLocale) is never referenced anywhere in the iOS app. CompanionView therefore falls back to Locale.current (system locale) on iOS, breaking date formatting and language-sensitive labels whenever the user's chosen in-app language differs from the device locale. The fix is fully portable: add .environment(\.locale, LanguageManager.shared.preferredLocale) to the CompanionView call in WaiHomeView, or wrap with LanguageManagedRoot. MacDateFormatting itself (DesignSystem.swift:161-185) uses only Locale, DateFormatter, and LanguageManager.SupportedLanguage — zero macOS-only symbols.

perDomain.title: App updates
perDomain.summary: The 'App updates' domain is entirely NOT_APPLICABLE on iOS. macOS uses Sparkle (SPUStandardUpdaterController + BetaChannelUpdaterDelegate + RecordingAwareUpdateUserDriverDelegate) for background update checks, a beta-channel opt-in toggle, recording-aware deferral, and a manual 'Check for Updates' entry point in both the app menu and Settings. None of these concepts exist on iOS: the App Store / TestFlight manage all update delivery at the OS level, no in-app update sheet can be triggered, and the beta channel is managed through TestFlight groups rather than an in-app preference. The only update-adjacent surface present on both platforms is the static version/build display in Settings, which is already at full parity. There are zero portable gaps in this domain.

perDomain.title: In-app language switching
perDomain.summary: iOS has zero in-app language switching today. The shared WaiComputerKit LanguageManager contains the complete core (setLanguage, UserDefaults persistence, preferredLocale, SupportedLanguage enum, first-launch seeding), but the iOS app entry point (WaiComputerApp.swift) never instantiates or injects it — so the locale environment injection, the settings picker, the onboarding language slide, and the bilingual text helper are all absent. All six gaps are PORTABLE: the shared kit code needs no changes; the work is purely iOS UI wiring and one new onboarding slide. The critical prerequisite is capability 1 (LanguageManager instantiation + root locale injection in WaiComputerApp.swift); all other capabilities depend on it. Total estimated effort: 2 S items + 1 M item + 3 S items wired together in one PR.

gaps.name: LanguageManager shared core (setLanguage, current, preferredLocale, UserDefaults persistence, SupportedLanguage enum)
gaps.risk: Without this wiring no other in-app language capability can work on iOS; it is the prerequisite for every capability below.

verdict.capabilityName: LanguageManager shared core (setLanguage, current, preferredLocale, UserDefaults persistence, SupportedLanguage enum)
verdict.reason: The gap is real and confirmed. A complete grep of the entire ios/ tree returns zero matches for LanguageManager, languageManager, preferredLocale, LanguageManagedRoot, SupportedLanguage, waiUserLanguage, and setLanguage. The iOS WaiComputerApp (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/WaiComputerApp.swift:204-220) creates only AppState and injects nothing language-related. The macOS app instantiates LanguageManager.shared at WaiComputerMacApp.swift:80 and injects it at lines 93-94. The shared implementation (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift) imports only Foundation, SwiftUI, and os — all fully available on iOS. The one mention of "Sparkle" at line 25 is inside a doc-comment string, not a real import or API call. No macOS-only symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CGEventTap, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap, CATap, MenuBarExtra, NSApp, etc.) appear anywhere in LanguageManager.swift. LanguageManagedRoot is also in the shared kit and ready for iOS use. The fix is to add @StateObject private var languageManager = LanguageManager.shared to WaiComputerApp and wrap ContentView() with .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale).environmentObject(languageManager), exactly mirroring the macOS wiring.

gaps.name: Settings row: in-app language picker (Picker with English/Russian, persists to UserDefaults)
gaps.risk: Depends on LanguageManager being injected at root (capability above). Low risk once that prerequisite is met.

verdict.capabilityName: Settings row: in-app language picker (Picker with English/Russian, persists to UserDefaults)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Settings/SettingsView.swift (lines 36–148) contains only Account, Recording, Data, Integrations, and About sections. A full grep of the entire iOS source tree finds zero references to LanguageManager, AppLanguagePicker, waiUserLanguage, or setLanguage. The macOS AppLanguagePicker.swift (all 40 lines) imports only SwiftUI and WaiComputerKit and uses exclusively cross-platform symbols: Picker, .pickerStyle(.menu), @EnvironmentObject, LanguageManager.SupportedLanguage, and LanguageManager.setLanguage. No macOS-only symbols (NSPanel, NSWindow, NSStatusItem, NSWorkspace, CGEvent, CATap, Sparkle, AX APIs, Carbon hotkeys, menu bar, dock) appear anywhere in the file. LanguageManager itself (shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift) is pure Swift/Foundation/SwiftUI with no platform conditionals and is already linked by the iOS target (WaiComputerApp.swift imports WaiComputerKit). The only wiring absent on iOS is: (1) injecting LanguageManager.shared as an .environmentObject in WaiComputerApp.swift and (2) adding an AppLanguagePicker row to the iOS SettingsView. Both are achievable with SwiftUI alone.

gaps.name: Root locale injection so SwiftUI Text(key) re-resolves on language change without restart
gaps.risk: Bundled with the LanguageManager wiring capability; trivially safe.

verdict.capabilityName: Root locale injection so SwiftUI Text(key) re-resolves on language change without restart
verdict.reason: Gap confirmed. macOS wires LanguageManager.shared.preferredLocale into the SwiftUI environment at WaiComputerMacApp.swift:93 via .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) on the WindowGroup body. iOS WaiComputerApp.swift:208-219 does not instantiate LanguageManager, does not inject \.locale, and has no equivalent path (grep across all iOS .swift files returns zero hits for LanguageManager, preferredLocale, and LanguageManagedRoot). The shared kit already provides two ready-made mechanisms at shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift: LanguageManager.shared.preferredLocale (line 79) and the LanguageManagedRoot convenience wrapper (lines 128-140). Both import only Foundation, SwiftUI, and os — all fully available on iOS. iOS already imports WaiComputerKit at WaiComputerApp.swift:2, so no new dependency is needed. No iOS-unavailable symbols (NSWindow, Sparkle, CGEventTap, CATap, Carbon, RegisterEventHotKey, SUUpdater, AXUIElement, NSStatusItem, etc.) appear anywhere in the locale injection code path. The fix is a one-liner: add @StateObject private var languageManager = LanguageManager.shared and .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) to the WindowGroup body in WaiComputerApp.swift, or wrap ContentView() with LanguageManagedRoot.

gaps.name: First-launch language seeding via WAIDownloadRegion Info.plist key (seeds AppleLanguages, chooses ru vs en default)
gaps.risk: If the iOS App Store release does not have a regional variant, WAIDownloadRegion will be absent and the manager will default to English — acceptable and consistent with current iOS behaviour.

verdict.capabilityName: First-launch language seeding via WAIDownloadRegion Info.plist key
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed on two independent levels. First, the iOS Info.plist (/ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Info.plist) contains no WAIDownloadRegion key; the macOS Info.plist (/macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Info.plist:48-49) stamps it as $(WAI_DOWNLOAD_REGION) at archive time. Second, no iOS Swift file references LanguageManager at all (grep of the entire ios/ tree returns zero hits), so LanguageManager.init — which contains the resolveFirstLaunchDefault and seedAppleLanguagesIfNeeded calls at lines 62-76 of LanguageManager.swift — is never executed on iOS. LanguageManager.swift itself imports only Foundation, SwiftUI, and os; uses only UserDefaults, Bundle, and Logger; and contains zero macOS-only symbols (the word "Sparkle" appears only in a doc comment at line 24). The fix is purely additive: (1) add WAIDownloadRegion to the iOS Info.plist, stamped by the iOS archive pipeline analogously to macOS, and (2) construct LanguageManager.shared in WaiComputerApp.init and wire .environment(\.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) on the root WindowGroup content, mirroring WaiComputerMacApp.swift lines 80 and 93-94.

gaps.name: Onboarding language-picker slide (dictation language selection during onboarding flow)
gaps.risk: Must verify that LanguagePickerView in WaiComputerKit has no NSColor/AppKit references before reusing it on iOS; if it does, a new iOS-specific List picker is needed. DictationLanguageStore and DictationManager must also be injected into the iOS onboarding environment.

verdict.capabilityName: Onboarding language-picker slide (dictation language selection during onboarding flow)
verdict.reason: The gap is confirmed. iOS OnboardingPage.swift has cases welcome/record/transcribe/permission/voiceSetup only — no .languages case (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingPage.swift:3-8). iOS OnboardingView.swift renders only OnboardingSlide or OnboardingVoiceSetupSlide (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/OnboardingView.swift:42-49). Language selection on iOS exists only as the legacy @AppStorage("transcriptionLanguage") Picker in post-onboarding TranscriptionSettingsView (ios/.../SettingsView.swift:212), not as an onboarding step. There is one real blocking dependency: OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:48 uses Color(NSColor.windowBackgroundColor), an AppKit symbol unavailable on iOS. Additionally, DictationLanguageStore lives only in macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Dictation/ — it is not in shared/WaiComputerKit and not imported in any iOS file. These are mechanical blockers, not architectural ones: replace NSColor.windowBackgroundColor with Color(.systemBackground) (UIKit equivalent), move DictationLanguageStore to the shared kit (it has no AppKit imports — pure Foundation + UserDefaults), and the slide and picker become pure SwiftUI. LanguagePickerView itself contains zero macOS-only symbols. The proposed iOS targets (OnboardingPage.swift, OnboardingView.swift, new OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift) are the correct intervention points. Classification remains PORTABLE.
hiddenPlatformDeps: NSColor.windowBackgroundColor (OnboardingLanguagesSlide.swift:48 — replace with Color(.systemBackground) for iOS)
DictationLanguageStore is macOS-target-only (macos/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/Features/Dictation/DictationLanguageStore.swift) — must be moved to shared/WaiComputerKit before iOS can use it

gaps.name: OnboardingL10n bilingual text helper (resolves English vs Russian strings for onboarding copy based on current LanguageManager selection)
gaps.risk: DictationHotkey label extensions in OnboardingL10n reference macOS hotkey names (Right Option, Fn/Globe) that are meaningless on iOS; those extension methods should be excluded from the iOS target or guarded with #if os(macOS).

verdict.capabilityName: OnboardingL10n bilingual text helper
verdict.reason: The gap is real: OnboardingL10n.swift is absent from the iOS onboarding directory (ios/WaiComputer/WaiComputer/App/Onboarding/) and iOS OnboardingPage.swift (line 1–64) contains only hardcoded English strings with no reference to LanguageManager. LanguageManager itself lives in shared/WaiComputerKit/Sources/WaiComputerKit/Localization/LanguageManager.swift and is fully portable (Foundation + SwiftUI only), but iOS never imports or uses it. The core OnboardingL10n.text(_:_:language:) and language(for:) helpers at macos/.../OnboardingL10n.swift:10–29 depend only on LanguageManager.SupportedLanguage and are portable in isolation. However, the DictationHotkey extension at OnboardingL10n.swift:32–62 is a blocking dependency: DictationHotkey is defined in GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413 which is guarded by Carbon, Cocoa, ApplicationServices, and IOKit.hid imports (lines 1–4), and uses kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand (Carbon virtual-key constants, line 447–451) plus NSEvent.ModifierFlags (line 456) and CGEventTap (line 468) — all macOS-only symbols unavailable on iOS. The proposed target ios/.../OnboardingL10n.swift cannot be a straight copy of the macOS file; the DictationHotkey extension must be excluded (iOS has no dictation hotkey picker) or placed behind a macOS conditional compilation block before the file can land on iOS.
hiddenPlatformDeps: Carbon (kVK_RightOption, kVK_Option, kVK_RightCommand, kVK_Function via GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:1-2,447-451)
DictationHotkey type itself — defined in GlobalHotkeyManager.swift:413, inseparable from its macOS-only host file without refactoring

synthesis.executiveSummary: iOS has solid parity on the core data and recording loop. The shared WaiComputerKit (models, APIClient, RecordingBackupStore, CompanionView, WebSocketManager, BillingClient, LanguageManager) means every network and data-layer behavior is automatically shared, and iOS already ships working Library list/detail, Trash, folder create, live recording, import, background sync, MCP connect, summary settings, and a first-class Wai companion tab. iOS is NOT structurally behind; the gaps fall into three themes. (1) A large set of macOS-only features that are genuinely impossible on iOS - system-wide push-to-talk dictation (global hotkeys, CGEvent text-injection, NSPanel overlay, Accessibility TCC), system-audio CATap, Sparkle updates, menu-bar/dock - all correctly platform-limited or N/A. (2) One pervasive missing foundation, in-app language switching: the shared LanguageManager is never instantiated on iOS, which simultaneously blocks the settings language picker, bilingual strings across Library/Recording/Search/Settings/Onboarding/Billing, and locale injection into the companion - wiring it is the single highest-leverage fix. (3) A long tail of leaf-screen polish that is purely SwiftUI plus shared-kit work (magic-link/forgot-password auth, billing read/cancel UI, Telegram connect, appearance theme, speaker rename/delete/merge, export/share, summary progress, search-tab wiring, Apps feature). The one true product decision is iOS billing: the macOS web-checkout path cannot ship on the App Store, so upgrade/purchase needs a StoreKit 2 vs external-link-entitlement choice even though the surrounding read-only billing UI is portable.
synthesis.parityScore: ~58% of macOS capabilities are present on iOS today. Basis: across the 11 audited domains there are ~284 total macOS capabilities; ~123 are already at parity (sum of alreadyParityCount: app-shell 31, onboarding 10, library 40, recording 18, search 12, settings 14, dictation 4, apps 2, billing 1, companion-wai 12, updates 6, localization 0). Counting the platform-limited/not-applicable gaps as legitimately not owed on iOS (~33 capabilities, mostly the entire dictation + system-audio + Sparkle + dock/menu-bar surface) lifts the achievable parity to roughly 80% - i.e. of everything iOS can reasonably have, about four-fifths exists, and the remaining ~50 portable gaps plus the billing decision are the build list.

portableGaps.name: Wire LanguageManager into iOS app root (instantiate + inject + locale override + first-launch WAIDownloadRegion seeding)
portableGaps.plan: 1) Add @StateObject private var languageManager = LanguageManager.shared to WaiComputerApp. 2) On the WindowGroup body add .environmentObject(languageManager) and .environment(.locale, languageManager.preferredLocale) (mirrors WaiComputerMacApp.swift:80,93-94) so Text(key) re-resolves without restart. 3) Add WAIDownloadRegion to ios Info.plist stamped from build variable WAI_DOWNLOAD_REGION exactly as macOS Info.plist:48-49 so LanguageManager.init resolveFirstLaunchDefault/seedAppleLanguagesIfNeeded run. platformLimited.name: Two-phase pre-auth/post-auth onboarding (hotkey + dictation-sandbox pre-auth content)
platformLimited.reason: The two-phase split exists only to host macOS-exclusive content: the hotkey slide configures a global push-to-talk key (GlobalHotkeyManager: NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents, CGEvent.post, Accessibility TCC) and the sandbox slide exercises CGEvent text-insertion. iOS has no global hotkey, no Accessibility TCC, no cross-app text injection. The deferral of post-auth voice setup is portable, but the pre-auth content is blocked; the iOS single-phase flow already covers everything iOS can offer.

platformLimited.name: Settings: Dictation on/off feature-enable toggle
platformLimited.reason: Toggles DictationManager.isFeatureEnabled which gates GlobalHotkeyManager (Carbon, IOKit.hid, NSEvent global monitor, AXIsProcessTrusted) + AudioEngineHost prewarm + DictationOverlayPanel (NSPanel) + TextInserter (NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post into NSRunningApplication). The entire system-wide-dictation premise is architecturally impossible on iOS.

platformLimited.name: Settings: Dictation post-filter (LLM cleanup) toggle
platformLimited.reason: The iOS toggle exists and persists server-side but controls nothing: iOS has no DictationManager/TextInserter/cleanupDictation runtime. The pipeline it gates depends on NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post(.cgSessionEventTap) + NSRunningApplication + Accessibility TCC, all iOS-unavailable. It is a dangling preference until an iOS in-app dictation feature is designed.

platformLimited.name: Settings: push-to-talk hotkey picker
platformLimited.reason: DictationHotkey uses Carbon kVK keycodes + NSEvent.ModifierFlags and configures GlobalHotkeyManager (NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents, CGEvent.post, AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions). iOS has no system-wide modifier-key interception and the physical keys (Right Option/Command/Fn) don't exist on iPhone.

platformLimited.name: Settings: hands-free hotkey picker (double-tap to start)
platformLimited.reason: Same macOS-only stack as the push-to-talk picker (Carbon keycodes, NSEvent global/local monitors, NSRunningApplication, NSPanel overlay). No iOS equivalent for choosing a global modifier key that fires dictation from any foreground app.

platformLimited.name: Settings: System Audio (CATap) permission row + test/grant
platformLimited.reason: SystemAudioGate/SystemAudioCapture are os(macOS)-gated in the shared kit. The feature depends on the macOS Core Audio HAL (CATapDescription, AudioHardwareCreateProcessTap/AggregateDevice, macOS 14.2+) which has no iOS counterpart - iOS provides no OS API to tap other processes audio output.

platformLimited.name: Settings: microphone permission status row (macOS implementation)
platformLimited.reason: The macOS permissionRow is bound to MacPrivacySettings.openMicrophone (NSWorkspace), restartForPermissionRefresh (NSApp.terminate), the staleNeedsRestart TCC restart state (AXIsProcessTrusted) and AVCaptureDevice authorization. The concept itself is portable - an iOS-native row using AVAudioApplication + UIApplication.openSettingsURLString is in portableGaps; only the macOS code is platform-limited.

platformLimited.name: scenePhase active: dictation/permission refresh half
platformLimited.reason: iOS already resumes pending sync on active (parity). The missing half is dictationManager.refreshPermissionState (GlobalHotkeyManager: Carbon/CGEventTap/NSWorkspace/IOKit.hid) and refreshPermissionStatus to MacInputPermission.postEventStatus (AXIsProcessTrusted). iOS has no global hotkey or input-monitoring TCC permission to re-poll.

platformLimited.name: Accent color preference wired to scene tint (macOS color construction)
platformLimited.reason: The .tint() and AppStorage wiring is portable, but MacAccentChoice.tintColor/.color (DesignSystem.swift:86-111) are built entirely with Color(nsColor:) and NSColor.controlAccentColor, AppKit-only with no UIColor equivalent. The color-construction layer must be rewritten per platform; the rewritten iOS appearance/accent work is captured in portableGaps.

platformLimited.name: Global push-to-talk hotkey (background, into any app)
platformLimited.reason: GlobalHotkeyManager imports Cocoa/Carbon/IOKit.hid/ApplicationServices; uses NSEvent.addGlobalMonitorForEvents + Carbon kVK + AXIsProcessTrusted. iOS apps cannot observe global input events while backgrounded - an absolute platform constraint requiring a different interaction model entirely.

platformLimited.name: Hands-free dictation mode (double-tap toggle continuous)
platformLimited.reason: Trigger detection is built on NSEvent global/local monitors, Carbon keycodes, double-tap timing in GlobalHotkeyManager, NSRunningApplication targeting, and Accessibility TCC - none available on iOS.

platformLimited.name: Floating always-on-top overlay panel
platformLimited.reason: DictationOverlayPanel subclasses NSPanel and relies on NSWindow.Level.statusBar, CollectionBehavior.canJoinAllSpaces, hidesOnDeactivate, NSHostingView, NSScreen.main. iOS sandbox forbids placing a window above other apps or the system status bar; there is no process-level floating panel.

platformLimited.name: Text insertion into other apps (clipboard + simulated Cmd+V via CGEvent.post)
platformLimited.reason: TextInserter uses NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post(tap .cgSessionEventTap) + NSRunningApplication + Accessibility TCC. iOS sandboxing prohibits posting synthetic keyboard events to other processes; there is no equivalent of CGEvent.post.

platformLimited.name: Accessibility + mic permission flow (TCC prompts, legacy TCC migration, stale-needs-restart)
platformLimited.reason: Built on AXIsProcessTrusted(WithOptions), NSEvent global monitor, tccutil shell commands via Process(), and NSWorkspace.openApplication + NSApp.terminate relaunch. iOS has no Accessibility TCC, no tccutil, no programmatic self-terminate, and permission changes take effect immediately (no stale-restart state). The mic subset already exists on iOS.

platformLimited.name: Dictation feature enable/disable toggle (gates hotkey + AudioEngineHost prewarm)
platformLimited.reason: The didSet calls applyHotkeyAvailability which starts/stops GlobalHotkeyManager + AudioEngineHost and depends on DictationOverlayPanel (NSPanel), NSRunningApplication, NSSound, TextInserter - all macOS-only.

platformLimited.name: Push-to-talk hotkey picker (Right Option/Left Option/Right Command/Fn/Ctrl+Option)
platformLimited.reason: DictationHotkey is defined inside the macOS-only GlobalHotkeyManager with Carbon keycodes + NSEvent.ModifierFlags. The physical modifier keys and system-wide interception API do not exist on iOS.

platformLimited.name: Hands-free hotkey picker (separate key or double-tap)
platformLimited.reason: Same DictationHotkey/Carbon/NSEvent global-monitor/Accessibility TCC stack as the PTT picker; no iOS global keyboard hook surface.

platformLimited.name: AI post-filter (cleanup) toggle has no iOS runtime
platformLimited.reason: The toggle is present and server-synced on iOS but functional only with an in-app dictation pipeline. The macOS pipeline (cleanupDictation to TextInserter via CGEvent.post + NSPasteboard + NSRunningApplication, gated by global hotkey) cannot be ported; making the toggle effective on iOS requires a new platform-specific dictation design.

platformLimited.name: Session token prefetching/vault for push-to-talk dictation
platformLimited.reason: The RealtimeTranscriptionSessionConfigVault subcomponent is portable, but its driver - prefetch 20s before a global hotkey fires - is bound to GlobalHotkeyManager (Carbon/NSEvent global monitor/NSPanel/Accessibility TCC). iOS has no system-wide PTT hotkey to pre-warm against; a portable iOS variant would be a new SwiftUI-button-triggered design, not a port.

platformLimited.name: Dictation history stats display (macOS view)
platformLimited.reason: The macOS DictationHistoryView is AppKit-bound (NSPasteboard, MacDateFormatting, OnboardingL10n, macOS-only DictationHistoryStore). The underlying stats/store are pure Swift and ARE included in the portable History-screen port - only the macOS view file is platform-limited.

platformLimited.name: Recovery text file when paste fails
platformLimited.reason: saveRecoveryText body is pure Foundation but it can only ever be called from the macOS TextInserter failure path (NSPasteboard + CGEvent.post into the frontmost app). Without inter-app text insertion (impossible on iOS) the recovery file is meaningless.

platformLimited.name: Dictation onboarding - hotkey picker slide
platformLimited.reason: OnboardingHotkeyPickerSlide iterates DictationHotkey.allCases (Carbon keycodes / NSEvent.ModifierFlags) and calls DictationManager.updateHotkey to GlobalHotkeyManager. iOS has no global keyboard hook or physical PTT modifier keys.

platformLimited.name: Dictation onboarding - sandbox try-it-now slide
platformLimited.reason: Takes a DictationManager (macOS-only: GlobalHotkeyManager Carbon/CGEvent/NSEvent, DictationOverlayPanel NSPanel, TextInserter). iOS has no push-to-talk concept; a portable equivalent requires a fully new iOS-native dictation flow.

platformLimited.name: Billing app-return refresh on scene active
platformLimited.reason: The refresh hook is portable SwiftUI but is triggered only by the checkout flow setting billingCheckoutRefreshPending, which on macOS opens the checkout URL via NSWorkspace.shared.open. Until iOS billing/checkout exists and uses UIApplication.open, the trigger the refresh depends on is iOS-unavailable. Gated on the billing-upgrade architecture decision.

notApplicable.name: Accessibility permission status row (global hotkey prereq)
notApplicable.reason: Gates GlobalHotkeyManager via AXIsProcessTrusted(). iOS has no Accessibility TCC trust model, no global hotkey API, and no cross-app event injection - the capability the row gates does not exist on iOS.

notApplicable.name: Dictation re-run setup / reveal in Finder / reset TCC permissions recovery actions
notApplicable.reason: All three target macOS-specific failure modes: resetOnboardingForSetupRerun (MacAppState only), NSWorkspace.activateFileViewerSelecting (Finder reveal), and tccutil reset via Process() + NSWorkspace.openApplication/NSApp.terminate restart. iOS has no TCC.db, no tccutil, no Process, and cannot self-terminate; these failure modes do not exist on iOS.

notApplicable.name: Settings: Sparkle beta/update controls
notApplicable.reason: App Store/TestFlight manage all iOS update delivery at the OS level; no in-app update sheet can be triggered and the beta channel is a TestFlight group, not an in-app preference.

notApplicable.name: Settings: dock-icon toggle
notApplicable.reason: iOS has no Dock; the concept of showing/hiding a Dock icon is meaningless on the platform.

notApplicable.name: Settings: payment-mode debug toggle
notApplicable.reason: Gates a web-checkout flow prohibited on the iOS App Store for digital goods; the underlying flow it toggles cannot ship on iOS.

notApplicable.name: Menu-bar dictation shortcut (last-dictation popover)
notApplicable.reason: Bound to MenuBarExtra (macOS-only SwiftUI scene) + NSPasteboard + NSApplication.terminate + MacPresentationCoordinator + DictationHistoryStore/DictationManager. iOS has no persistent ambient status-bar surface to host a last-dictation preview.

notApplicable.name: App updates domain (Sparkle background checks, beta-channel opt-in, recording-aware deferral, manual Check-for-Updates)
notApplicable.reason: Entire domain is OS-managed on iOS via App Store/TestFlight. SPUStandardUpdaterController + BetaChannelUpdaterDelegate + RecordingAwareUpdateUserDriverDelegate have no iOS analog; the only update-adjacent surface (version/build display) is already at parity.

notApplicable.name: Menu bar extra / Dock management / TCC migration / NSMenu commands (app shell chrome)
notApplicable.reason: macOS-only AppKit chrome (MenuBarExtra, Dock, Sparkle auto-update, TCC migration, NSMenu CommandMenu). None of these surfaces exist on iOS.

needsDecision.name: iOS upgrade / purchase flow (period picker + price + Upgrade button)
needsDecision.question: How should iOS users upgrade to Pro, given the macOS web-checkout (Stripe/Tinkoff via NSWorkspace.open) cannot ship for digital goods on the App Store? The verdicts confirm the surrounding UI is technically portable, but the payment mechanism is a real product/compliance decision.
options: StoreKit 2 in-app purchase (new App Store products, server-side receipt validation, entitlement sync to the existing backend) - App-Review-compliant but cannot reuse the Stripe/Tinkoff path and adds backend IAP work

External Purchase Link entitlement (StoreKit ExternalPurchaseLink, region-restricted, requires Apple approval) to keep the existing web checkout - lighter backend work but limited availability and stricter review
Ship iOS as read-only billing (status/usage/cancel only) and direct users to upgrade on web/macOS - fastest, avoids IAP entirely, no in-app purchase path on iOS

needsDecision.name: Billing region picker on iOS
needsDecision.question: Should the Global/RU region picker exist on iOS at all? It feeds the web-checkout flow that App Review Guideline 3.1.1 prohibits, so its presence depends on the upgrade-flow decision above.
options: Drop the region picker on iOS entirely (region inferred server-side from WAIDownloadRegion sync) if iOS uses StoreKit IAP
Keep it only if the External Purchase Link entitlement is approved and the web checkout is permitted
Show it read-only as informational region/currency display tied to the post-login region sync

needsDecision.name: Apps feature entry point and scope on iOS
needsDecision.question: MacAppsView is currently DEAD CODE on macOS (no SidebarSection.apps, no wiring) and the feature is fully absent on iOS. Should the user-created mini-apps feature be surfaced on iOS at all, and if so where, before investing in the full grid/detail/items UI?
options: Defer until the feature is validated and wired on macOS first (avoid building an unproven surface twice)
Build on iOS as a Settings sub-screen (low-commitment entry point, easy to hide)
Build as a first-class tab matching the macOS sidebar-section intent (highest visibility, largest churn)

needsDecision.name: Search entry point on iOS
needsDecision.question: SearchView is fully built but unreachable. Should it be a 5th MainTabView tab (matching macOS sidebar prominence) or a .searchable surface on the Library tab (more iOS-idiomatic, avoids tab-index migration)?
options: 5th tab - closest to macOS but requires migrating the persisted selectedTab AppStorage index so existing users don't mis-route
Library .searchable - idiomatic iOS, no tab-index churn, but lower discoverability and loses the standalone search-mode picker placement
Toolbar search button on Library that pushes SearchView - middle ground, no tab migration
